title: Admin password reset (POST /users/:id/reset-password) does not bump nbf — access token remains valid up to 7 days post-reset
description: The admin endpoint POST /users/:id/reset-password revokes all refresh tokens but never calls jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(). SEC-019 relies on nbf bump to invalidate existing access tokens after a password change. In production, JWT_ACCESS_TTL is unset and JWT_EXPIRES_IN defaults to 7d (docker-compose.prod.yml:121 and .env.production.example:60), so the effective access token lifetime is 7 days. An access token stolen before the admin reset remains fully valid for up to 7 days after the admin changed the password — the primary defence scenario for admin resets. Self-service changePassword also omits bumpUser. Only AuthService.resetPassword (token-based flow) correctly calls bumpUser.
root_cause: jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(userId) was added to deactivation paths (SEC-020) and the token-based reset flow (AUTH-019) but was forgotten in the admin reset endpoint and self-service changePassword.
impact: An attacker who stole a valid access token before an admin reset retains full API access for up to 7 days in production. This defeats the primary goal of admin-forced password resets (credential rotation after compromise).
suggested_fix: Add `await this.jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(userId);` after `await this.refreshTokenService.revokeAllForUser(userId);` at line 1129 in users.service.ts (resetPassword). Also add to changePassword (around line 1046 after revokeAllForUser). Alternatively, define a private helper `async invalidateAllTokens(userId)` that calls both and use it consistently across all password-changing paths.
acceptance_criteria: After admin POST /users/:id/reset-password, any previously issued access token for that user receives 401 on the next request
After user PATCH /me/change-password, any previously issued access token for that user receives 401 on the next request
Integration test: issue token, reset password via admin endpoint, assert token returns 401
notes: Production access TTL confirmed: docker-compose.prod.yml sets JWT_EXPIRES_IN=7d; auth.service.ts getAccessTtl() returns JWT_ACCESS_TTL||JWT_EXPIRES_IN||'15m'. No JWT_ACCESS_TTL is set in any prod template.

title: Self-service changePassword (PATCH /me/change-password) does not bump nbf — old access tokens remain valid up to 7 days
description: changePassword revokes all refresh tokens but never bumps the per-user nbf in Redis. An attacker who acquired the old access token (e.g., from a stolen localStorage on a shared device) continues to use it until it naturally expires. Under the production config where JWT_EXPIRES_IN=7d and JWT_ACCESS_TTL is unset, this window is 7 days. The nbf mechanism was specifically designed for this use case (SEC-019) but was not wired up to self-service password changes.
root_cause: Same root cause as SEC-001: nbf bump not called after password credential rotation.
impact: A user who changes their password to regain control of a compromised account (e.g., after noticing unauthorized activity) leaves the attacker's stolen access token valid for up to 7 days.
suggested_fix: Add `await this.jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(userId);` after `await this.refreshTokenService.revokeAllForUser(userId);` at line ~1046 in users.service.ts (changePassword).
acceptance_criteria: After PATCH /me/change-password, any previously issued access token for that user returns 401
Integration test: issue token, change password, assert token returns 401 on next request

title: LoginDto missing @MaxLength — unbounded login and password fields enable bcrypt DoS
description: Both the login and password fields in LoginDto have no @MaxLength constraint. The POST /auth/login endpoint calls bcrypt.compare() on the submitted password. bcrypt with cost=12 is intentionally slow; submitting a multi-megabyte password string will block the event loop for seconds per attempt. The global bodyLimit of 1 MiB (main.ts) caps the body size but still permits strings up to ~1 MB which are expensive to hash. The per-IP throttle (5/min) slows this but does not prevent a targeted DoS across distributed source IPs.
root_cause: LoginDto was never updated with @MaxLength constraints; only @MinLength was added.
impact: An attacker can submit a very long password string to POST /auth/login, causing bcrypt to block the Node.js event loop for an extended period per request, potentially causing DoS. Even with throttling, a single request with a near-1MB password causes measurable latency.
suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(72)` to both the login and password fields in LoginDto. 72 bytes is bcrypt's internal limit; longer inputs are silently truncated by bcrypt anyway. Alternatively use @MaxLength(128) for login and @MaxLength(72) for password.
acceptance_criteria: POST /auth/login with a 10KB+ password returns 400 immediately without calling bcrypt
POST /auth/login with a 10KB+ login returns 400 immediately

title: RefreshTokenDto missing @MaxLength — unbounded refresh token in POST /auth/refresh
description: Both RefreshTokenDto.refreshToken and LogoutDto.refreshToken lack @MaxLength. The refresh flow passes this value to SHA-256 hashing, which is fast and unbounded. While not a bcrypt-level DoS risk, applying @MaxLength is a defence-in-depth measure that prevents surprisingly large payloads from even reaching the service layer. The refresh cookie path is already length-capped (cookies have browser limits) but the body path is not.
root_cause: MaxLength constraint not applied to opaque token fields in auth DTOs.
impact: Minor — SHA-256 on large strings is fast but wastes CPU. Not a significant DoS vector at the bodyLimit (1 MiB).
suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(256)` to both refreshToken fields (a 48-byte base64url token is 64 chars; 256 provides generous headroom for variations).
acceptance_criteria: POST /auth/refresh with a 10KB refreshToken body field returns 400

title: CreateCommentDto and UpdateCommentDto missing @MaxLength on content field
description: The content field in CreateCommentDto and UpdateCommentDto has no @MaxLength constraint. An authenticated user can submit a very large comment payload that would be stored verbatim in the database, consuming disk space and memory when returned in comment listings. The global bodyLimit of 1 MiB limits individual requests but an attacker can fill the comments table with 1 MiB entries across multiple requests.
root_cause: MaxLength constraint not applied to the comment content field.
impact: Authenticated users can pollute the database with large comment entries, causing storage exhaustion and slow queries on task detail pages. Lower risk due to authentication requirement.
suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(10000)` (or a domain-appropriate limit) to the content field in both CreateCommentDto and UpdateCommentDto.
acceptance_criteria: POST /comments with a content > 10000 chars returns 400
PATCH /comments/:id with a content > 10000 chars returns 400

title: CreateProjectDto.description, icon, managerId, sponsorId missing @MaxLength
description: Four fields in CreateProjectDto lack @MaxLength: description (free text, can be arbitrarily large), icon (emoji or string), managerId and sponsorId (should be @IsUUID, not @IsString). The description and icon fields can receive arbitrarily long strings up to the 1 MiB body limit and be persisted in the database. The managerId and sponsorId fields accept any string rather than a UUID, potentially causing unexpected Prisma behavior on invalid values.
root_cause: MaxLength and UUID validation were not applied to optional fields in CreateProjectDto.
impact: Authenticated users with projects:create permission can store large project descriptions and icons. managerId/sponsorId accept arbitrary strings which Prisma may reject at the DB level with a 500 rather than a clean 400.
suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength to description (e.g., 5000 chars), icon (e.g., 10 chars for emoji), and replace @IsString() with @IsUUID('4') for managerId and sponsorId.
acceptance_criteria: POST /projects with description > 5000 chars returns 400
POST /projects with managerId = 'not-a-uuid' returns 400

title: CreateTaskDto.description and tags fields missing @MaxLength
description: The description field in CreateTaskDto has no @MaxLength. Similarly, the tags array field has no per-item or total-count limit. Large task descriptions are stored in the database and returned in list responses, potentially causing memory and bandwidth issues. The tags array could be used to store large amounts of data.
root_cause: MaxLength/ArrayMaxSize constraints were not applied to optional free-text and array fields in CreateTaskDto.
impact: Authenticated users can create tasks with very large descriptions, impacting database storage and API response sizes.
suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(10000) to description. Add @ArrayMaxSize(20) and @MaxLength(50, { each: true }) to tags.
acceptance_criteria: POST /tasks with description > 10000 chars returns 400
POST /tasks with tags containing more than 20 items returns 400

title: CreateLeaveDto.reason and targetUserId missing @MaxLength and @IsUUID
description: The reason field in CreateLeaveDto has no @MaxLength, allowing large text storage in leave records. The targetUserId field uses @IsString() instead of @IsUUID(), accepting arbitrary strings instead of enforcing the UUID format documented in the example. A non-UUID targetUserId would cause a Prisma error at the DB layer rather than a clean 400 validation error.
root_cause: MaxLength and @IsUUID not applied to leave creation DTO.
impact: Large reason strings stored in leave records; non-UUID targetUserId causes unhandled Prisma errors returned as 500.
suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(2000) to reason. Replace @IsString() with @IsUUID('4') on targetUserId.
acceptance_criteria: POST /leaves with reason > 2000 chars returns 400
POST /leaves with targetUserId = 'not-a-uuid' returns 400

title: CreateLeaveDto.leaveTypeId uses @IsString() instead of @IsUUID()
description: The leaveTypeId field is documented as a UUID-like identifier but validated only with @IsString(). The example 'lt-cp-001' suggests this may intentionally be a non-UUID short identifier; however, if leaveTypeId is a database UUID FK (as the schema likely defines), only @IsUUID() is appropriate. If it really is a non-UUID slug, then it should have a @MaxLength and @Matches constraint. Either way, unbounded @IsString() is insufficient.
root_cause: leaveTypeId validation not aligned with the actual data type used in the database schema.
impact: A long leaveTypeId causes a Prisma findUnique miss (404) at worst, but could also cause slow regex/DB lookups with pathological inputs.
suggested_fix: If leaveTypeId is a UUID FK in Prisma schema: replace @IsString()/@IsNotEmpty() with @IsUUID('4'). If it is a slug: add @MaxLength(64) @Matches(/^[a-z0-9-]+$/) or similar.
acceptance_criteria: POST /leaves with leaveTypeId of invalid format returns 400 instead of 404
notes: Need to verify leaveTypeId DB type in schema.prisma to confirm whether it should be UUID or slug.

title: CreateEpicDto.description and CreateMilestoneDto.description missing @MaxLength
description: The description fields in CreateEpicDto and CreateMilestoneDto have no @MaxLength constraint. While CreateMilestoneDto similarly lacks it, this applies to all major resource DTOs where optional free-text description fields are not length-capped. Storage abuse by authenticated users is the primary risk.
root_cause: Pattern of omitting @MaxLength on optional description fields across multiple DTOs.
impact: Authenticated users can store large descriptions in epics and milestones, consuming database storage.
suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(5000) to description in CreateEpicDto and CreateMilestoneDto.
acceptance_criteria: POST /epics with description > 5000 chars returns 400
POST /milestones with description > 5000 chars returns 400

title: Metrics endpoint token comparison uses !== (timing-unsafe) instead of timingSafeEqual
description: The METRICS_TOKEN comparison uses the JavaScript !== operator, which is a timing-unsafe string comparison. This allows a timing side-channel attack to oracle the token value character-by-character. The Swagger basic-auth in main.ts uses timingSafeEqual (main.ts:37) but this pattern was not applied here. Although METRICS_TOKEN is a static secret and HTTPS in production makes timing attacks very difficult, consistency with the existing safeEqual() helper is appropriate.
root_cause: The safeEqual() helper defined in main.ts (line 34-38) was not reused in the metrics controller.
impact: Theoretical timing side-channel to brute-force the METRICS_TOKEN value. Practically negligible over HTTPS, but violates defence-in-depth.
suggested_fix: Refactor the comparison to use crypto.timingSafeEqual: `if (!authorization || !timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(authorization), Buffer.from(expected))) { throw ... }`. Extract safeEqual() from main.ts into a shared utility.
acceptance_criteria: METRICS_TOKEN comparison uses timingSafeEqual instead of !==

title: METRICS_TOKEN not documented in env templates — metrics endpoint is open by default
code_evidence:
/**
 * OBS-011 — Exposes /api/metrics in Prometheus text format.
 *
 * Auth logic:
 *  - If METRICS_TOKEN is unset (dev/test), the endpoint is fully open.
 *  - If METRICS_TOKEN is set, the request MUST include an Authorization header
 *    matching "Bearer <METRICS_TOKEN>". Otherwise → 401.
 *
 * We mark the controller @Public() to bypass the global JwtAuthGuard
 */
description: METRICS_TOKEN is referenced in the controller but absent from both .env.example and .env.production.example. An operator following the env templates to configure production will not set METRICS_TOKEN, leaving /api/metrics fully unauthenticated. The endpoint exposes HTTP request counts, routes, status codes, and latencies — aggregated telemetry that reveals the application's internal route structure and error rates to any unauthenticated requester. This is also not protected by the global JwtAuthGuard due to @Public().
root_cause: METRICS_TOKEN was added to the controller but not to the env template files used for operator onboarding.
impact: Unauthenticated access to production telemetry: route enumeration, traffic patterns, error rate inference. Low impact on its own but adds to reconnaissance capability.
suggested_fix: Add `METRICS_TOKEN=` (with a generation command) to .env.example and .env.production.example with a prominent note that it is required in production. Also add to docker-compose.prod.yml's API environment block.
acceptance_criteria: METRICS_TOKEN is documented in .env.example
METRICS_TOKEN is documented in .env.production.example
GET /api/metrics returns 401 in production when no token is provided

title: Nginx config has no HSTS header and no HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect
description: The nginx config defines only a port 80 server block with no HTTPS listener, no HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect, and no Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header. The comment 'behind host nginx SSL proxy' suggests TLS may terminate upstream, but the shipped nginx.conf would serve production traffic over plain HTTP if deployed without an additional TLS-terminating proxy. Even if TLS terminates upstream, HSTS should be emitted by the application to instruct browsers to enforce HTTPS for future visits. Without HSTS, browsers will happily follow HTTP links to the application.
root_cause: TLS configuration was deferred to an upstream proxy (host nginx/HAProxy), and no HSTS header was added to compensate.
impact: If deployed without an upstream TLS proxy, all traffic including JWT access tokens (in Authorization headers) and refresh cookies transmit over cleartext HTTP. Even with upstream TLS, absence of HSTS leaves users vulnerable to SSL stripping on first visit.
suggested_fix: Add `add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" always;` to the server block. If TLS terminates upstream, add a dedicated HTTPS server block with SSL certs or redirect port 80 to 443 within the Docker stack.
acceptance_criteria: curl -I https://prod-host/ includes Strict-Transport-Security header
HTTP requests to port 80 are redirected to HTTPS (301)
notes: Architecture comment 'behind host nginx SSL proxy' implies TLS may be handled externally; this finding assumes the shipped nginx.conf is the complete deployment config. Impact is blocking if nginx.conf is the sole ingress.

title: docker-compose.prod.yml uses deprecated ALLOWED_ORIGINS env var instead of canonical CORS_ORIGIN
code_evidence:
# CORS
      ALLOWED_ORIGINS: ${ALLOWED_ORIGINS:?ALLOWED_ORIGINS is required}
description: The production compose file passes ALLOWED_ORIGINS to the API container, but the canonical variable name is CORS_ORIGIN as documented in .env.production.example and cors.config.ts (which reads CORS_ORIGIN first, ALLOWED_ORIGINS as deprecated alias). An operator who sets only CORS_ORIGIN (following .env.production.example instructions) will trigger the required-boot assertion error because docker-compose.prod.yml demands ALLOWED_ORIGINS be set. This creates a confusing, inconsistent operator interface.
root_cause: docker-compose.prod.yml was not updated when CORS_ORIGIN was established as the canonical variable in SEC-012.
impact: Operator confusion: the .env.production.example says CORS_ORIGIN, the compose file says ALLOWED_ORIGINS. No security bypass (cors.config.ts handles both), but a misconfigured deployment could use an unintended CORS origin list.
suggested_fix: Update docker-compose.prod.yml line 130 to: `CORS_ORIGIN: ${CORS_ORIGIN:-${ALLOWED_ORIGINS}}` to accept both, or migrate exclusively to `CORS_ORIGIN: ${CORS_ORIGIN:?CORS_ORIGIN is required}`.
acceptance_criteria: docker-compose.prod.yml uses CORS_ORIGIN as the primary variable name

title: X-Forwarded-Proto forwarded as $http_x_forwarded_proto without sanitization — spoofable by external client
description: The nginx config sets X-Forwarded-Proto to $http_x_forwarded_proto, which is the value of the incoming X-Forwarded-Proto request header from the client. If a client sends a forged X-Forwarded-Proto: https header on an HTTP request, nginx will forward it unchanged to the API. This is harmless if the API does not make security decisions based on X-Forwarded-Proto, but should be $scheme (the actual protocol nginx received the request on) to prevent proto-header injection.
root_cause: nginx was configured to forward client-supplied headers rather than using the nginx-determined $scheme variable.
impact: Low — the API appears to use X-Forwarded-Proto primarily for logging. However if any future middleware logic (e.g., HTTPS enforcement checks) relies on this header, a client could spoof it to appear as HTTPS while communicating over HTTP.
suggested_fix: Change `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $http_x_forwarded_proto;` to `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;` in both /api and / location blocks.
acceptance_criteria: nginx forwards X-Forwarded-Proto: http for plain HTTP requests regardless of client-supplied headers

title: CI workflow hardcodes DB password and JWT secret in plaintext — appropriate for ephemeral CI but not using GitHub Secrets
code_evidence:
postgres:
        image: postgres:18
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: orchestr_a
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: orchestr_a_dev_password
          POSTGRES_DB: orchestr_a_v2_test
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
description: The CI workflow hardcodes `orchestr_a_dev_password` as the Postgres password and `test-jwt-secret-key` as the JWT secret in plaintext in the YAML file. While these are ephemeral CI values used only for test services, they are committed to the repository and will appear in git history indefinitely. If an operator ever reuses these values in a staging or production environment, those credentials are publicly known. The docker-publish.yml and docker-compose.yml also use the same default password.
root_cause: CI DB credentials were not parameterized as GitHub Secrets, likely because they are considered dev-only throwaway values.
impact: Directly: none — these are ephemeral CI containers. Indirectly: if an operator copies these values for convenience into a non-CI environment, those environments are compromised. The password `orchestr_a_dev_password` is a known weak default.
suggested_fix: Move CI DB passwords to GitHub repository secrets (`${{ secrets.CI_DB_PASSWORD }}`) to establish a good hygiene pattern. Alternatively, document clearly that these values must NEVER be reused outside ephemeral CI.
acceptance_criteria: No plaintext passwords appear in ci.yml (use ${{ secrets.CI_DB_PASSWORD }} or similar)
notes: The .env files containing real dev secrets are correctly gitignored. This finding applies only to the CI workflow YAML file itself.

title: AppController GET / exposes docs and internal API endpoint list publicly without auth
description: The root GET / endpoint is @Public() and returns the application name, version, status, and a structured list of key API endpoints including the path to the Swagger docs (/api/docs). This is an information-disclosure endpoint that aids reconnaissance. The Swagger endpoint path leakage is especially relevant if SWAGGER_ENABLED=true is ever set without noticing this public disclosure point. The version field (2.0.0) helps attackers correlate known vulnerabilities.
root_cause: Root endpoint was designed for developer convenience with no auth and includes rich metadata.
impact: Enables endpoint discovery and version fingerprinting without authentication. Low direct impact but reduces the work required for targeted attacks.
suggested_fix: Remove the `endpoints` object and `version` from the root response, or restrict the endpoint to authenticated requests. A minimal `{ status: 'operational' }` response is sufficient for health discovery while minimizing information disclosure.
acceptance_criteria: GET / does not return version information or endpoint listing
GET /api/health continues to function for container health checks

title: JWT_ACCESS_TTL undocumented in prod template — effective access token TTL is 7d not 15m as SEC-019 assumes
code_evidence:
# JWT token validity (formats: 1h, 8h, 1d, 7d, 30d)
JWT_EXPIRES_IN=7d
description: The .env.production.example sets JWT_EXPIRES_IN=7d without defining JWT_ACCESS_TTL. The auth.service.ts getAccessTtl() function resolves: JWT_ACCESS_TTL || JWT_EXPIRES_IN || '15m'. In production, this resolves to 7d as the access token TTL. Several security code comments assert '15min access TTL' (e.g., jwt-not-before.service.ts:33: 'the 15-min access TTL caps the residual exposure') — those comments are incorrect for the default production config. The security model of SEC-001/SEC-002 (and the nbf fail-open logic) assumes short-lived access tokens; with 7d TTLs the fail-open on Redis error means tokens are valid for 7 days even if Redis is down.
root_cause: JWT_ACCESS_TTL was added as a more granular control but not set in the production template; JWT_EXPIRES_IN was intended as the refresh token TTL but doubles as the access token TTL due to the fallback chain.
impact: The effective production access token TTL is 7 days, not 15 minutes. The nbf fail-open window is 7 days instead of 15 minutes. The SEC-001/SEC-002 findings above describe consequences of stolen tokens with this 7d window.
suggested_fix: Add `JWT_ACCESS_TTL=15m` (or 1h for a balance) to .env.production.example and docker-compose.prod.yml, separate from JWT_REFRESH_TTL=7d. Update code comments referencing the '15min' assumption.
acceptance_criteria: JWT_ACCESS_TTL is set independently in .env.production.example
JWT_EXPIRES_IN is used solely as the refresh token TTL or removed in favour of JWT_REFRESH_TTL
notes: docker-compose.prod.yml:121 confirms: JWT_EXPIRES_IN: ${JWT_EXPIRES_IN:-7d}. auth.module.ts uses the same fallback chain. No JWT_ACCESS_TTL appears in any prod template.

title: captureSnapshots uses local-TZ midnight for `date`, causing duplicate/wrong-day snapshots in prod (UTC)
description: The snapshot scheduler calls `new Date()` then `.setHours(0,0,0,0)`, which produces the *local-time* midnight. The prod server runs UTC, but the `date` column in `ProjectSnapshot` is typed as `@db.Date` and Prisma persists it using UTC date components. When the API process runs on a server whose TZ is anything other than UTC (e.g. Europe/Paris +01/+02), `startOfDay` is shifted: on a Paris server, midnight local = 23:00 the previous UTC day, so the snapshot lands on the wrong calendar date. The same value is used for the de-duplication query — if the stored row was created yesterday (UTC) but today (Paris), the de-dup misses it and a second snapshot is inserted, breaking the @@unique([projectId, date]) invariant or silently writing a duplicate via `skipDuplicates`.
root_cause: `setHours(0,0,0,0)` anchors midnight on the process local timezone rather than UTC, inconsistent with the `@db.Date` column semantics that Prisma resolves from UTC date components.
impact: In production (UTC, no drift), this is a no-op. On a non-UTC server (e.g. the Europe/Paris VPS), every snapshot is off by one calendar day; the de-duplication query also fails because the stored UTC date and the query date disagree, so the cron can insert a second snapshot for the same project on the same Paris day.
suggested_fix: Replace `setHours(0,0,0,0)` with `new Date(Date.UTC(now.getUTCFullYear(), now.getUTCMonth(), now.getUTCDate()))` to pin midnight to UTC, consistent with how `holidays.service.ts` and the leave year-window helpers construct their date boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: On a Europe/Paris host, `captureSnapshots` inserts a snapshot with `date = UTC midnight of the current Paris calendar day`
The de-duplication query finds the previously-inserted row on the same Paris calendar day and skips re-insertion
Unit test: mock `new Date()` to 2026-01-01T23:30:00Z (= Jan 2 in Europe/Paris), assert `startOfDay` equals 2026-01-01T00:00:00Z
notes: The leave-year-window helpers (parisYearWindow, and so on) consistently use Date.UTC / fromZonedTime. This one service is the outlier.

title: UsersService.update: userService delete+createMany runs outside the user.update transaction, leaving service memberships temporarily inconsistent
description: The service-membership sync (deleteMany + createMany) executes before the `user.update`. These three operations are issued as three separate database statements with no wrapping `$transaction`. If `createMany` or `user.update` fails after `deleteMany` has committed, the user's service memberships are wiped and never restored. A concurrent read between `deleteMany` and `createMany` also observes a user with no service memberships — which can yield wrong RBAC perimeter calculations (leave approvals, task scoping) for the duration of the gap.
root_cause: No `$transaction` wraps the three sequential Prisma calls that form a single logical mutation.
impact: A failure between `deleteMany` and `createMany` leaves the user with zero service memberships, silently breaking all perimeter-based access-control checks until an admin manually repairs the record. A concurrent read during the gap returns an incorrect (empty) membership set.
suggested_fix: Wrap the entire service-sync + user.update block inside `this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`, using `tx.userService.deleteMany`, `tx.userService.createMany`, and `tx.user.update`. This matches the pattern already used in `users.service.ts` line 948 for the full-erasure path.
acceptance_criteria: All three DB mutations (deleteMany, createMany, user.update) are inside a single $transaction
If user.update throws (e.g. unique constraint on login), the deleteMany is rolled back and the original service memberships are preserved
Integration test: inject a failing user.update and assert the user's service memberships remain unchanged after the error

title: resetPassword: user.update and token.usedAt mark are non-atomic — token can be double-consumed under concurrent requests
description: The password update and the token invalidation (`usedAt = now()`) are two sequential, non-transactional DB writes. Between the two writes, a second concurrent call with the same token passes the `usedAt !== null` check (line 555: token was found, `usedAt` is still null) and proceeds to hash and apply a new password, setting the user's password to the second caller's value. The first caller's password is then overwritten and the intended password reset fails. This is a classic double-submit / replay window on the reset token.
root_cause: No `$transaction` wraps `user.update` and `passwordResetToken.update`, leaving a window where the token appears unconsumed to a concurrent reader.
impact: An attacker who intercepts or guesses a valid reset token and replays it before it is marked used can overwrite the victim's password to their own value, achieving full account takeover.
suggested_fix: Wrap both writes in a single `$transaction`. Additionally, consider changing the token lookup to a conditional update (`updateMany({ where: { token: tokenHash, usedAt: null } })`) and checking the affected count to detect concurrent consumption atomically.
acceptance_criteria: user.update and passwordResetToken.update (usedAt) execute inside a single $transaction
Concurrent simultaneous calls with the same token result in exactly one password update; the second call receives an error
Test: two concurrent resetPassword calls with the same token — only one succeeds
notes: The generateResetToken method correctly invalidates prior tokens atomically. This resetPassword path does not apply the same discipline.

title: addDependency allows two orphan tasks (projectId = null) to be linked, violating the 'same project' invariant
description: The cross-project guard uses a strict JavaScript inequality (`!==`). When both `task.projectId` and `dependsOnTask.projectId` are `null`, the comparison evaluates `null !== null` which is `false`, so the guard passes silently. This means any two orphan tasks (meetings, cross-cutting tasks) can be linked as dependencies regardless of whether they have any business relationship. The orphan-task feature is intentional per CLAUDE.md, but linking arbitrary orphan tasks as dependencies creates invisible coupling that persists even when one of the tasks is later attached to a project via `attachToProject`.
root_cause: The `!==` operator does not distinguish `null !== null` (both orphans) from `null !== 'project-uuid'` (different projects), treating both cases identically while only one should be an error.
impact: Any user can link any two orphan tasks as dependencies, potentially creating circular-dependency chains or polluting the dependency graph with semantically unrelated tasks. If a linked orphan is later attached to a project, the cross-project dependency remains, bypassing the same-project invariant for that pair.
suggested_fix: Add an explicit null check: `if (task.projectId === null || task.projectId !== dependsOnTask.projectId)`. Orphan tasks should not participate in dependency relationships (they have no project scope to define what 'same scope' means), or the check should be `task.projectId !== null && task.projectId === dependsOnTask.projectId` (i.e. same non-null project).
acceptance_criteria: addDependency(orphanTask1, orphanTask2) throws BadRequestException
addDependency(projectTask, orphanTask) throws BadRequestException
addDependency(projectTask1, projectTask2_same_project) succeeds as before

title: PersonalTodosService.create: count-then-create is non-atomic — concurrent requests can push todo count above 20
description: The 20-item hard cap is enforced by a `count` query followed by a `create` outside any transaction. Two concurrent requests by the same user both read `count = 19`, both pass the `>= 20` check, and both proceed to `create`, resulting in 21 items. CLAUDE.md documents this as a hard invariant ('Personal Todos: hard-coded 20-item limit'). The race is realistic via rapid UI interaction or API tooling.
root_cause: The count-check and the create are two separate, unprotected DB operations with no locking or atomic guard.
impact: The 20-item invariant documented in CLAUDE.md can be violated, resulting in more than 20 todos per user. Downstream code (UI rendering, any report that assumes the cap) may behave incorrectly.
suggested_fix: Wrap the count + create in a `$transaction` and re-check the count inside the transaction, or use a DB-level constraint (`CHECK (count(*) <= 20)` via a trigger, or an application-level unique index with a sequence). The simplest correct fix is: `await this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { const count = await tx.personalTodo.count(...); if (count >= MAX_TODOS) throw ...; return tx.personalTodo.create(...); })`.
acceptance_criteria: Two concurrent create requests from the same user at count=19 result in exactly one success and one 400 error
After concurrent requests, the DB row count for that user is exactly 20
Existing sequential behaviour (creating up to 20 succeeds, the 21st fails) is preserved
notes: The auto-cleanup in findByUser runs before create is called, reducing the window slightly, but it does not close the race.

title: getLeaveBalance uses server-local new Date().getFullYear() — on a Europe/Paris server the 'current year' is wrong between Dec 31 23:00 and Jan 1 00:00 UTC
description: All leave accounting is anchored on Europe/Paris (`LEAVE_TIMEZONE = 'Europe/Paris'`). The `parisYearWindow` helper correctly converts Paris year boundaries to UTC. However, `getLeaveBalance` derives `currentYear` from `new Date().getFullYear()`, which uses the *process local timezone* rather than Paris time. On a UTC server this is a no-op. On a Europe/Paris server it would appear correct. But the real edge case: on Jan 1, between 00:00 and 01:00 Paris time (= Dec 31 23:00–Jan 1 00:00 UTC), `new Date().getFullYear()` on a UTC server returns the *old* year (e.g. 2025) while Paris employees are already in the new year (2026). Their balance is computed against 2025's allocations and 2025 leaves, showing the wrong year's balance.
root_cause: `new Date().getFullYear()` does not account for the Europe/Paris timezone when determining which calendar year is 'current' for French public-sector employees.
impact: For employees using the leave portal between 23:00 and 00:00 UTC on New Year's Eve (= 00:00–01:00 Jan 1 Paris), `getLeaveBalance` returns 2025's balance instead of 2026's, showing a potentially stale or confusing balance during a narrow but deterministic window.
suggested_fix: Replace `new Date().getFullYear()` with `Number(formatInTimeZone(new Date(), 'Europe/Paris', 'yyyy'))` using the already-imported `date-fns-tz` helpers, consistent with how `parisDayKey` and `parisYearWindow` derive the Paris calendar context.
acceptance_criteria: At 2026-12-31T23:30:00Z (= 2027-01-01 00:30 Paris), getLeaveBalance returns year=2027
At 2026-01-01T00:30:00Z (= 2026-01-01 01:30 Paris), getLeaveBalance returns year=2026
Unit test: mock Date.now() to Dec 31 23:30 UTC and assert currentYear=next year
notes: The impact is a 1-hour window per year. Low frequency, but deterministic and incorrectly computes a financial variable (leave balance).

title: importLeaves bulk import: getHolidayKeySet is called inside the $transaction — Prisma nested reads on the outer prisma client inside a tx client context may bypass the tx isolation
description: `importLeaves` wraps its loop in `this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`. Inside the transaction body, the code calls `this.getHolidayKeySet()`, which internally calls `this.holidaysService.findByRange()` — a method that uses `this.prisma` (the root Prisma client, not `tx`). This is the same pattern the code intentionally avoids in `create()` (commented 'Référentiel statique, lu hors transaction'), but here it is inside the transaction. Using the root prisma client inside a transaction body means the holiday read is on a different connection and is not subject to the transaction's isolation level. This is architectural inconsistency but not a correctness bug in practice (holidays are reference data), EXCEPT that it means the holiday read does NOT hold the `tx` connection — creating N extra connections per imported leave row inside the transaction, risking connection pool exhaustion for large CSV imports.
root_cause: `getHolidayKeySet` uses `this.prisma` unconditionally and cannot be passed a transaction client; its caller inside the tx body therefore establishes a separate connection per invocation.
impact: For large CSV imports, each row opens an additional DB connection for the holiday read inside the outer transaction. This risks Prisma connection pool exhaustion (pool default: 5–10 connections) or significant performance degradation. The import is not technically wrong (holidays are read-only reference data), but the repeated per-row query inside the transaction is wasteful.
suggested_fix: Pre-fetch the holiday key set once BEFORE the transaction (as done in create()/update()), computing the union of all rows' date ranges, and pass the result into the loop. This removes the per-row nested read and is consistent with the documented pattern.
acceptance_criteria: getHolidayKeySet is called at most once per importLeaves invocation, before the $transaction block
The resulting holidayKeys set covers the union of all rows' [startDate, endDate] ranges
A 100-row CSV import triggers exactly 1 holiday lookup, not 100

title: tasks.update: assigning an epicId or milestoneId that belongs to a different project than the task's (possibly updated) projectId is not validated
description: The `create` method (lines 153–182) correctly validates that `epic.projectId === projectId` and `milestone.projectId === projectId`. The `update` method validates only that the epic/milestone *exist* but does NOT check that they belong to the same project as the task (either the task's existing `projectId` or the newly supplied `projectId` in the DTO). An attacker or UI bug can therefore link a task in project A to an epic from project B, violating the referential integrity that the create path enforces. The DB schema may not carry a composite FK for this cross-entity constraint.
root_cause: The update validation was written as a subset of create validation, omitting the cross-project membership check for epicId and milestoneId.
impact: A task can be in project A while referencing an epic or milestone from project B. This corrupts project-level reporting (epic progress, milestone completion counts) and may cause 'Projet introuvable' or incorrect scope responses in the project detail endpoint.
suggested_fix: In the update method's epicId validation block, after confirming the epic exists, add: `const effectiveProjectId = projectId ?? existingTask.projectId; if (epic.projectId !== effectiveProjectId) throw new BadRequestException("L'epic n'appartient pas au même projet");`. Apply the same pattern for milestoneId.
acceptance_criteria: PATCH /tasks/:id with epicId belonging to a different project returns 400
PATCH /tasks/:id with milestoneId belonging to a different project returns 400
PATCH /tasks/:id with epicId null (clearing the epic) still succeeds
Existing valid updates (epic in the same project) are unaffected

title: validateImport (tasks): end <= start is classified as a warning, but create() rejects end < start as an error — inconsistent boundary semantics
description: The `validateImport` dry-run marks equal-start-and-end-date tasks as `warning` (not `error`). The `create()` method (line 210) explicitly allows equal dates: `if (end < start)` (strict less-than). The validation preview therefore incorrectly flags valid single-day tasks (`start == end`) as warnings, confusing operators. The preview message 'antérieure ou égale' is also misleading since the actual constraint only rejects `end < start`.
root_cause: The validation preview uses `<=` while the runtime constraint uses `<`.
impact: Single-day tasks in the CSV are shown as warnings in the preview but actually import successfully, causing operator confusion and potentially causing valid imports to be abandoned.
suggested_fix: Change line 1673 to `if (end < start)` to match the runtime constraint and update the message to 'La date de fin est antérieure à la date de début'.
acceptance_criteria: validateImport: a task with startDate == endDate is classified as 'valid', not 'warning'
validateImport: a task with endDate < startDate is classified as 'error'

title: canValidate: active delegation check is scope-agnostic — any active delegate can validate any leave, regardless of whether the delegator manages the leave's owner
description: The delegation fallback in `canValidate` returns `true` for any active delegation where `delegateId === validatorId`, regardless of which `delegatorId` granted it. A delegate appointed by manager A (who manages service X) is thus authorized to approve leave requests for employees in service Y, even if the delegator has no authority over service Y. The earlier checks (assigned validatorId, APPROVE_LEAVES + service scope) correctly implement scope. The delegation fallback discards scope entirely.
root_cause: The delegation lookup omits a join back to the delegator's authority scope.
impact: A delegate granted by any manager effectively gains global leave approval authority for the duration of the delegation, regardless of the delegating manager's perimeter. This violates the 'RESPONSABLE scope périmètre' invariant noted in MEMORY.md.
suggested_fix: Extend the delegation query to also check that the delegator (the `delegatorId`) would have authority over the leave's owner. Specifically: for each active delegation where `delegateId = validatorId`, check that `canManageLeave(leave.userId, delegation.delegatorId, delegatorRole)` would be true — meaning the delegator is in the leave owner's perimeter.
acceptance_criteria: A delegate from manager A (service X only) cannot approve leave of an employee in service Y
A delegate from an ADMIN-role manager (leaves:manage_any) can approve any leave
Existing behaviour for correctly-scoped delegations is preserved
notes: The MANAGE_DELEGATIONS permission gate on createDelegation limits who can create delegations, partially mitigating this. But anyone with that permission (MANAGER+ via template) can create a delegation that grants globally-scoped approval power to the delegate.

title: TaskRACI.userId has no FK to users — dangling reference on user deletion
code_evidence:
model TaskRACI {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId    String
  userId    String
  role      RACIRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  // Relations
  task Task @relation(fields: [taskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([taskId, userId, role])
  @@map("task_raci")
}
description: TaskRACI.userId is declared as a plain String with no Prisma relation and no matching FK constraint in any migration. The init migration (20251116093059) only creates `task_raci_taskId_fkey` pointing at tasks; no FK to users was ever added. When a user is physically deleted or anonymised, their task_raci rows are not cleaned up: the userId column retains a stale UUID. The hardDelete function in users.service.ts deletes taskAssignee rows but has no mention of TaskRACI.
root_cause: The model was authored without a FK relation on userId, and no subsequent migration added one; users.hardDelete() cleans TaskAssignee but not TaskRACI.
impact: After a user deletion (physical or anonymise-shell), task_raci rows retain a userId pointing at a non-existent or anonymised user. RACI query results (who is Accountable/Responsible for a task) silently surface orphaned IDs. Any JOIN to users for display will return null, making the RACI matrix appear to have anonymous entries.
suggested_fix: Add a Prisma relation to users with onDelete: SetNull (make userId nullable) or onDelete: Cascade. Add a FK via a migration: `ALTER TABLE "task_raci" ADD CONSTRAINT "task_raci_userId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("userId") REFERENCES "users"("id") ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE;`. Make userId nullable in the schema and migration. Also add `await tx.taskRACI.deleteMany({ where: { userId: id } });` inside the hardDelete transaction (or rely on Cascade once the FK is added).
acceptance_criteria: psql query `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM task_raci tr LEFT JOIN users u ON tr.userId = u.id WHERE u.id IS NULL;` returns 0 after a user deletion
Integration test: delete a user who has RACI entries → task_raci rows are either deleted (Cascade) or userId set to null (SetNull)
notes: Confirmed: init migration line 303 shows only task_raci_taskId_fkey on task_raci, no userId FK. users.hardDelete at line 956 deletes taskAssignee but never TaskRACI.

title: UserSkill.validatedBy is an untyped String? with no FK to users — dangling reference risk
code_evidence:
model UserSkill {
  userId      String
  skillId     String
  level       SkillLevel
  validatedBy String?
  createdAt   DateTime   @default(now())
  updatedAt   DateTime   @updatedAt

  // Relations
  user  User  @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  skill Skill @relation(fields: [skillId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@id([userId, skillId])
  @@map("user_skills")
}
description: UserSkill.validatedBy is typed as String? with no Prisma relation and no FK constraint in the database. The init migration (20251116093059) confirms that user_skills has only two FK constraints: userId → users and skillId → skills. No FK for validatedBy was added in any of the 74 migrations. When the user referenced by validatedBy is deleted, the validatedBy column retains the stale UUID indefinitely.
root_cause: The field was added as a plain String? annotation without a FK, likely as a lightweight reference, and no later migration promoted it to a proper FK.
impact: After a user (skill validator) is deleted, any UserSkill row with that user's id in validatedBy becomes a dangling reference. Skill validation history is silently corrupted: any display that resolves validatedBy to a user name will silently return null or fail.
suggested_fix: Add `validatedById String? @map("validated_by_id")` with a proper Prisma relation `validatedByUser User? @relation(..., onDelete: SetNull)` and rename the column. Alternatively keep it as an opaque string but rename to `validatedBySnapshot` and populate it with the user's name at write time (frozen snapshot, same pattern as audit_logs.actorLabel). The snapshot approach avoids a new FK migration on a composite-PK table.
acceptance_criteria: Either: `psql`: `\d user_skills` shows a FK constraint on the validator column pointing to users
Or: the column is renamed to a snapshot field and populated with the user's display name at skill-validation time, never looked up via JOIN
notes: init migration lines 249-257 show user_skills table with validatedBy TEXT; lines 378-381 add only userId and skillId FKs.

title: LeaveTypeConfig.remove() checks only leaves count — silently cascade-deletes LeaveBalance rows
description: The remove() function guards physical deletion by checking whether the LeaveTypeConfig has associated Leave rows (via _count.leaves). If the count is 0, it proceeds to delete. However LeaveBalance has a FK to LeaveTypeConfig with onDelete: Cascade (schema.prisma line 710). A LeaveTypeConfig with zero leaves but non-zero leave_balances (annual entitlement records) will be physically deleted, silently cascade-deleting all the leave_balance rows for all users and the global default for that type. No check for `leaveBalances` count is included.
root_cause: The guard was written for the primary child relation (Leave) but did not extend to the secondary child (LeaveBalance), whose FK carries Cascade instead of Restrict.
impact: An admin deleting an unused leave type (no past leaves taken) silently erases all configured annual entitlement balances. For example, a custom leave type seeded with global and per-user balances at year-start can be destroyed by a single admin delete if no leave has been taken yet. The erasure is not reflected in any audit entry and is not reversible without a backup.
suggested_fix: Add `leaveBalances: true` to the _count include in remove(). In the guard block, treat a positive balance count the same as a positive leave count — deactivate instead of delete: `if (existing._count.leaves > 0 || existing._count.leaveBalances > 0) { ... deactivate ... }`. Alternatively change LeaveBalance's FK to Restrict and handle deletion explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Integration test: create a LeaveTypeConfig with 0 leaves but N leave_balances → calling remove() deactivates rather than deletes, and all leave_balances remain
psql: after the fix, a direct DELETE on leave_type_configs with referencing balance rows fails with FK violation if Restrict is chosen
notes: Schema line 710 confirms onDelete: Cascade on LeaveBalance.leaveType FK. Service line 152-155 selects only _count.leaves. Leave_balance cascade confirmed in migration 20260321105758.

title: USER_DELETED audit entry committed before erasure transaction — stale audit if tx rolls back
description: The USER_DELETED audit log entry is written at line 916 in a self-contained, committed transaction managed by AuditPersistenceService (as documented by the comment at line 912: 'Plain await, non-transactional'). The erasure transaction follows at line 948. If the erasure transaction fails for any reason — most importantly the concurrent-audit-insert race described below — the audit_logs row permanently records the user as deleted while the user row still exists. The race: (1) hardDelete checks authoredAuditRows = 0 at line 904; (2) a concurrent request (e.g. the user logs in via another tab) inserts a new audit_logs row; (3) USER_DELETED is committed; (4) tx.user.delete fails with FK violation (ON DELETE NO ACTION on audit_logs.actorId); the transaction rolls back but the immutable USER_DELETED entry cannot be reversed.
root_cause: The audit entry is written outside and before the operational transaction to avoid passing a transaction client to AuditPersistenceService, matching the archive/unarchive pattern. But unlike archive/unarchive the subsequent operation can fail due to a DB-level FK race that the pre-check at line 904 cannot prevent.
impact: The immutable audit chain permanently shows USER_DELETED for a user who still exists, with no compensating SYSTEM entry. Subsequent reads of the audit trail for that user are misleading. The race window is narrow but non-zero: it requires a concurrent login (which appends audit_logs) between lines 904 and 997.
suggested_fix: Move the USER_DELETED audit log write to inside the erasure transaction, passing `tx` to `auditPersistence.log()`. AuditPersistenceService already supports a caller-supplied transaction client (documented at lines 104-110). This eliminates the race window entirely: if the tx rolls back, no audit row is committed.
acceptance_criteria: Integration test: simulate a concurrent audit insert between the check and the transaction — verify no orphan USER_DELETED log exists after a FK-triggered rollback
Code review: USER_DELETED auditPersistence.log() called with `tx` argument inside the $transaction lambda
notes: Line 912 confirms 'Plain await, non-transactional'. Line 916-936: audit committed. Line 948: erasure $transaction begins. The same pre-tx pattern in project deletion (projects.service.ts line 855) is protected by an earlier checkProjectDependencies(); the user path has no equivalent guard for the concurrent-audit-insert case.

title: DAT-021 hash-chain recompute is operator-manual with no CI/migration guard that it executed
code_evidence:
--   rowHash must be recomputed. That walk runs AFTER this migration via
--   scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts (advisory-lock + trigger-disable
--   inside one transaction, SYSTEM_BACKFILL-bracketed). See the runbook in the
--   DAT-021 closing commit body. Until the recompute runs, freshly inserted rows
--   are self-consistent (he hash with schemaVersion); pre-existing rows verify
--   only after the recompute.
description: Migration 20260526120000 adds the schemaVersion column to audit_logs and folds it into the computeRowHash input. Because schemaVersion was added via a catalog backfill (NOT a per-row UPDATE, so the immutability trigger is not triggered), every pre-existing row's prevHash/rowHash was computed without schemaVersion. The recompute script (scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts) must be run manually after this migration. No CI workflow step, no migration post-hook, and no integration test asserts that the recompute was executed. If the migration ran without the follow-up script, the stored rowHash for all pre-existing rows is stale and the chain does not verify against the current computeRowHash function.
root_cause: The DAT-021 design deliberately decoupled the migration (schema change) from the hash recompute (data change) to avoid a long-running DDL+UPDATE in a single migration, but provided no automation to guarantee the second step ran.
impact: Any audit chain verification that re-derives rowHash from stored fields and compares to the stored value will fail for all rows inserted before the DAT-021 migration was applied, if the recompute script was not run. This makes the tamper-evidence property of the chain unverifiable for the historical segment, undermining the Cour-des-Comptes auditability claim.
suggested_fix: Add a CI integration test that: (1) applies all migrations to a real PG DB, (2) inserts a seed row before the DAT-021 migration point via a partial migrate run, (3) finishes migrations, then (4) asserts that the full chain verifies. Alternatively, promote the recompute to a Prisma post-migrate hook or a documented, enforced deploy gate (checklist item with git-signed confirmation). Minimal: add a witness assertion in the existing dat021 int spec that verifies chain integrity over any seed rows.
acceptance_criteria: CI step exists that runs the chain-verification logic after `prisma migrate deploy` completes
OR: the recompute script is idempotent and runs automatically as part of the standard deploy flow (deploy-prod.sh or equivalent)
notes: Confirmed: no CI workflow file mentions 'recompute', 'schema-bump', or 'schemaVersion'. The witness script exists at apps/api/src/scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.witness.ts but is explicitly operator-manual (README line 78 says 'run against a throwaway DB').

title: scripts/backup-database.sh produces unencrypted .gz dumps with no restore-verification step
code_evidence:
docker exec "${CONTAINER_NAME}" pg_dump -U "${DATABASE_USER}" "${DATABASE_NAME}" > "${BACKUP_DIR}/${BACKUP_FILE}"
...
gzip "${BACKUP_DIR}/${BACKUP_FILE}"
...
find "${BACKUP_DIR}" -name "orchestr-a-backup-*.sql.gz" -type f -mtime +${RETENTION_DAYS} -delete
description: The repo-tracked backup script calls pg_dump and gzip-compresses the result. No GPG/AES encryption step is applied before writing to disk (confirmed: no mention of gpg, openssl, or ENCRYPT in the file). No restore verification step exists (no pg_restore into a test DB, no row-count assertion). The MEMORY note records that production uses a separate /opt/orchestra/scripts/backup-daily.sh (systemd timer) which is not in this repository tree, so its encryption posture cannot be audited from the repo. The in-repo script is the operator-documented recovery procedure and sets the backup hygiene expectation.
root_cause: The script was written to demonstrate basic pg_dump automation; encryption and verification were not added, which is a common gap in initial backup implementations.
impact: Backup files at rest are SQL plaintext compressed with gzip, readable by anyone with filesystem access to the backup directory. If the backup directory is on a network share, cloud storage bucket, or compromised host, all PII (user emails, names, leave records, audit logs) is immediately readable. Without restore verification, a backup may be silent-corrupted (truncated write, partial dump) and the error only surfaces during a real restore attempt.
suggested_fix: Add GPG symmetric encryption after gzip: `gpg --symmetric --cipher-algo AES256 --batch --passphrase-fd 0 <<< "$BACKUP_KEY" "${BACKUP_FILE}.gz"`. Store BACKUP_KEY in the environment/secret vault, not in the script. Add a smoke restore: `pg_restore --list ${BACKUP_FILE}.gz | wc -l` must be > 0. Alternatively use `pg_dump -Fc` (custom format) which is natively compressed and can be partially verified with `pg_restore --list`.
acceptance_criteria: backup-database.sh produces a file that is not readable as plain SQL without a decryption key
backup-database.sh exits non-zero if the produced file cannot be listed by pg_restore/gunzip
notes: This finding is scoped strictly to the in-repo script. The prod systemd backup script at /opt/orchestra/scripts/backup-daily.sh is not in this tree; its posture cannot be assessed here.

title: project_snapshots.progress has no DB-level floor CHECK (>= 0) — Decimal(5,2) admits negatives
code_evidence: progress           Decimal  @db.Decimal(5, 2) // 0.00 – 100.00 ; DAT-005 (Decimal exact arithmetic, no IEEE 754 drift)
description: The DAT-004 migration (20260527120000) adds progress BETWEEN 0 AND 100 checks on tasks and epics, but not on project_snapshots.progress. The DAT-005 comment acknowledges intentional headroom beyond 100 ('an analytics consumer that stores weighted scores >100 isn't broken'), so the upper bound of 100 is deliberately not constrained. However, the same migration family's pattern of applying a floor of >= 0 (as applied to leave_balances.totalDays, documents.size) was not applied here. Decimal(5,2) admits values down to -999.99.
root_cause: project_snapshots.progress was absent from the DAT-004 coverage list, likely because the intent was to defer the analytics headroom question; the floor check was not split from the ceiling check when the ceiling was intentionally omitted.
impact: A scheduler bug or a direct SQL INSERT could write a negative progress value (e.g. -0.01) to a snapshot row. The analytics queries that aggregate snapshot progress would produce nonsensical negative history with no DB-level rejection.
suggested_fix: Add `ALTER TABLE "project_snapshots" ADD CONSTRAINT "project_snapshots_progress_floor_ck" CHECK ("progress" >= 0);` in a follow-up migration.
acceptance_criteria: Integration test: INSERT INTO project_snapshots with progress = -1 fails with check_violation
INSERT with progress = 150 succeeds (intentional headroom preserved)
notes: DAT-005 comment at schema line 216 explicitly documents >100 headroom as intentional. The floor-only fix is non-controversial and does not conflict with that intent.

title: Event.recurrenceDay, TeleworkRecurringRule.dayOfWeek, and PredefinedTaskRecurringRule.dayOfWeek have no BETWEEN 0 AND 6 DB CHECK
code_evidence:
recurrenceDay          Int? // 0=Monday, 1=Tuesday, ..., 6=Sunday
  ...
  dayOfWeek   Int // 0=Monday, 1=Tuesday, ..., 6=Sunday
  ...
  dayOfWeek         Int? // 0=Monday..6=Sunday ; NULL pour MONTHLY_DAY
description: Three columns encode day-of-week as Int with the documented valid range 0–6. No DB-level CHECK constraint was added in the DAT-003/004 migration family or any subsequent migration. The DTO layer validates these values at the application layer, but a direct SQL INSERT or a buggy service path could write day value 7 or -1. All three columns are commented with their range but have no DB floor.
root_cause: The DAT-003/004 family focused on temporal range (startDate/endDate) and scalar bounds (progress, allocation, weight) but did not enumerate the smaller day-of-week columns.
impact: A value of 7 or -1 in recurrenceDay/dayOfWeek would cause recurrence expansion algorithms to malfunction (infinite loop or wrong date), silently producing incorrect scheduling outputs.
suggested_fix: Add three CHECK constraints in a single migration: `ALTER TABLE "events" ADD CONSTRAINT "events_recurrenceDay_ck" CHECK ("recurrenceDay" BETWEEN 0 AND 6); ALTER TABLE "telework_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "telework_recurring_rules_dayofweek_ck" CHECK ("dayOfWeek" BETWEEN 0 AND 6); ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "predefined_task_recurring_rules_dayofweek_ck" CHECK ("dayOfWeek" BETWEEN 0 AND 6);`
acceptance_criteria: Integration test: INSERT INTO events with recurrenceDay = 7 fails with check_violation
Integration test: INSERT INTO events with recurrenceDay = NULL passes (nullable column)
notes: All three columns documented as 0-6 range in schema comments but no DB CHECK found across all 74 migrations.

title: PredefinedTaskRecurringRule.weekInterval, monthlyOrdinal, monthlyDayOfMonth and Event.recurrenceWeekInterval have no DB-level range CHECKs
code_evidence:
weekInterval      Int       @default(1) // 1=weekly, 2=biweekly... pour WEEKLY uniquement
  recurrenceType    RecurrenceType @default(WEEKLY)
  monthlyOrdinal    Int? // 1..5 (5 = dernière occurrence du mois) pour MONTHLY_ORDINAL
  monthlyDayOfMonth Int? // 1..31 pour MONTHLY_DAY
  ...
  recurrenceWeekInterval Int?
description: Four columns carry documented numeric bounds in comments but no DB CHECK: weekInterval (>= 1, otherwise a 0-interval recurrence loops infinitely), monthlyOrdinal (1–5 per comment), monthlyDayOfMonth (1–31 per comment), Event.recurrenceWeekInterval (nullable Int, should be > 0 when set). No migration in the 74-migration history adds a CHECK on any of these columns.
root_cause: The DAT-004 migration family enumerated the bounded columns explicitly; these scheduling-specific bounds were not included in that pass.
impact: weekInterval = 0 would cause a recurrence expansion loop to produce infinite or incorrectly-dated occurrences. monthlyDayOfMonth = 32 or monthlyOrdinal = 6 would silently be accepted and cause expansion to skip months or produce wrong ordinal offsets.
suggested_fix: Add CHECKs in a migration: `ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "predefined_task_recurring_rules_weekinterval_ck" CHECK ("weekInterval" >= 1); ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "predefined_task_recurring_rules_monthlyordinal_ck" CHECK ("monthlyOrdinal" BETWEEN 1 AND 5); ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "predefined_task_recurring_rules_monthlyday_ck" CHECK ("monthlyDayOfMonth" BETWEEN 1 AND 31); ALTER TABLE "events" ADD CONSTRAINT "events_recurrenceweekinterval_ck" CHECK ("recurrenceWeekInterval" > 0);`
acceptance_criteria: Integration test: INSERT predefined_task_recurring_rules with weekInterval = 0 fails with check_violation
INSERT with monthlyDayOfMonth = 32 fails, INSERT with monthlyDayOfMonth = NULL passes
notes: weekInterval added in migration 20260330185549 with DEFAULT 1 but no CHECK. monthlyOrdinal and monthlyDayOfMonth added in 20260424124537 with no CHECK.

title: startTime/endTime string columns have no DB CHECK enforcing endTime >= startTime
code_evidence:
startTime              String? // Horaire de début optionnel (format HH:MM)
  endTime                String? // Horaire de fin optionnel (format HH:MM)
  ...
  startTime              String? // Format HH:MM
  endTime                String? // Format HH:MM
description: Six time-of-day columns (Task.startTime/endTime, Event.startTime/endTime, PredefinedTask.startTime/endTime) have their format checked by the DAT-013 migration (HH:MM regex). However, no DB CHECK enforces that endTime >= startTime within a given row. A task with startTime='17:00' and endTime='09:00' passes all current DB constraints. The DTO layer could enforce ordering but string comparison of HH:MM works for 00:00–23:59, making a DB check simple and unambiguous.
root_cause: DAT-013 focused solely on format validity; the intra-row ordering invariant was not included in scope.
impact: Tasks or events with inverted time windows pass DB validation and are persisted. Duration calculations (endTime - startTime) produce negative durations, which corrupts time-slot aggregation in planning views.
suggested_fix: Add a cross-column CHECK per table. String comparison of HH:MM format works correctly for 00:00–23:59: `ALTER TABLE "tasks" ADD CONSTRAINT "tasks_time_order_ck" CHECK ("startTime" IS NULL OR "endTime" IS NULL OR "endTime" >= "startTime");` (same pattern for events and predefined_tasks).
acceptance_criteria: Integration test: INSERT a task with startTime='17:00', endTime='09:00' fails with check_violation
INSERT with startTime='09:00', endTime=NULL passes (NULL is valid)
notes: DAT-013 migration confirms format CHECKs exist but contains no ordering constraint. HH:MM lexicographic comparison is a reliable ordering for this format.

title: ProjectMember.startDate/endDate has no date-ordering CHECK (endDate >= startDate)
code_evidence:
model ProjectMember {
  id         String    @id @default(uuid())
  projectId  String
  userId     String
  role       String
  allocation Int?
  startDate  DateTime?
  endDate    DateTime?
  createdAt  DateTime  @default(now())
description: The DAT-003 migration (20260527120000) adds endDate >= startDate CHECKs on leaves, projects, epics, telework_recurring_rules, leave_validation_delegates, and school_vacations. ProjectMember has optional startDate and endDate but is absent from that migration. An inverted membership period (startDate after endDate) is accepted by the DB.
root_cause: ProjectMember was not included in the DAT-003 scope, possibly because both dates are optional — however the NULL semantics of the existing CHECKs show this is handled correctly (NULL passes a CHECK).
impact: A project member record with startDate='2027-01-01' and endDate='2026-01-01' would pass DB validation. Membership overlap/activity queries that filter by date range would silently return wrong results.
suggested_fix: Add to a follow-up migration: `ALTER TABLE "project_members" ADD CONSTRAINT "project_members_dates_ck" CHECK ("endDate" >= "startDate");`
acceptance_criteria: Integration test: INSERT project_member with startDate='2027-01-01', endDate='2026-01-01' fails with check_violation
INSERT with startDate='2026-01-01', endDate=NULL passes
notes: DAT-003 migration covers six tables but not project_members. NULL semantics are handled in that migration's preamble and would apply here without changes.

title: User.roleId remains nullable — users without a role bypass RBAC entirely
description: User.roleId is nullable as a transitional state documented in contract-03 §9. The RBAC V4 migration (20260420120000) dropped the legacy `users.role` enum column and backfilled roleId. However, the column remains nullable in the current schema (git HEAD 0997b30e). Any user with roleId IS NULL has no role and no permissions, effectively becoming a zero-permission user. The comment 'passera NOT NULL après backfill stable' has no associated migration in the 74-migration history.
root_cause: The NOT NULL promotion step was deferred during the RBAC V4 migration to allow gradual rollout; the follow-up migration enforcing NOT NULL has not been written.
impact: A user record with roleId NULL (possible via a direct SQL INSERT, a test fixture, or an API bug in user creation) would pass all FK and NOT NULL constraints but have no permissions. PermissionsService would resolve an empty template for a null roleId, potentially allowing default behavior that bypasses RBAC. Also, any feature that filters users by role (analytics, permission matrix) would silently exclude null-role users.
suggested_fix: Add a migration: (1) verify 0 users have roleId IS NULL (`SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE roleId IS NULL`), (2) if clean: `ALTER TABLE "users" ALTER COLUMN "roleId" SET NOT NULL;`, (3) update schema.prisma to `roleId String` (non-optional). Also update the UserCreateDto to require roleId.
acceptance_criteria: psql: `\d users` shows roleId NOT NULL
Integration test: creating a user without roleId fails at the DB level
notes: Schema comment explicitly flags this as deferred; no later migration promotes to NOT NULL. This is a known pending item per contract-03 §9.

title: projects.findAll default limit is 1000 — effectively unbounded for admins
description: The default limit is 1000 and the hard ceiling is also 1000, so any caller without an explicit limit gets up to 1000 project rows per page. For ADMIN/RESPONSABLE/MANAGER roles (hasFullVisibility = true) this applies to the full projects table with eager-loaded members (take: 5 each), createdBy, manager, sponsor, clients, and all _count sub-selects. With 500 projects this generates a JOIN tree with 500×5 = 2500 ProjectMember rows hydrated per request.
root_cause: Default and ceiling limit values are both 1000; there is no differentiation between list-page use (needs ~20) and export use (needs all).
impact: A single request from an ADMIN role fetches up to 1000 projects with 4 JOINed relations each. At 500 projects, response payloads can exceed 1 MB and Prisma will hydrate thousands of related rows. Response times will scale super-linearly as the database grows.
suggested_fix: Lower the default limit to 20 and the hard ceiling to 200 for the standard list endpoint; provide a separate, authenticated export endpoint without a page limit. Or at minimum align with tasks.findAll's default of 10.
acceptance_criteria: GET /projects without explicit limit parameter returns at most 20 items
GET /projects?limit=201 is rejected with 400 or silently capped at 200
An export endpoint (CSV or JSON) exists for ADMIN with explicit ceiling
notes: tasks.findAll uses default 10 but ceiling 1000 — the ceiling issue is shared.

title: tasks.findAll hard ceiling is 1000 — same page can carry a very large payload
description: The default is 10 (good) but any caller can pass limit=1000 and receive up to 1000 tasks in a single page. Each task row includes the full assignee, all TaskAssignee rows (each with user fields), and four _count subqueries. With 1000 tasks and 5 assignees each, this hydrates 5000 TaskAssignee+User rows per request.
root_cause: Ceiling is set to 1000 which matches the default limit of the same function's fall-through (`limit || 1000`), allowing effective bypass of pagination.
impact: A single request with limit=1000 on a large project can transfer several hundred KB of JSON and require Prisma to hydrate thousands of nested rows. At scale this causes OOM on the Node process or multi-second query times.
suggested_fix: Lower the ceiling to 100 for the standard list. If a bulk export is needed add a dedicated endpoint that streams CSV directly from the DB without hydrating JS objects.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks?limit=101 is capped at 100
findAll safeLimit formula uses a ceiling of 100 not 1000

title: getTasksByProject is completely unbounded — no skip/take on all project tasks
description: This method returns every task for a project with no pagination (no take/skip). It includes dependencies (with dependsOnTask), assignees with user details, subtasks, and _count subqueries. A project with 500 tasks, each with 3 subtasks and 2 dependencies, produces a response of thousands of rows. This endpoint is called from the project detail page and task management views.
root_cause: No pagination was added to the project-scoped task list; the caller (frontend project detail page) is expected to receive all tasks at once.
impact: A large project with hundreds of tasks will cause multi-second Prisma query times and multi-MB JSON responses. The Node.js serialization of such a large include graph can exhaust heap memory.
suggested_fix: Add skip/take with a reasonable page size (50-100) and return a paginated result, or at minimum add a hard cap of 500 rows via take. The frontend should use virtual scrolling or lazy loading for large task lists.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks?projectId=X never returns more than 200 tasks in a single response
getTasksByProject adds a hard take cap or pagination parameters

title: getTasksByAssignee is completely unbounded — loads all tasks for a user with time entries
description: Returns all tasks ever assigned to a user (primary or multi-assignee) without any limit. Includes assignees list, _count subqueries for 4 relations, and all non-dismissal time entries. A user with 200 completed tasks each with 10 time entries generates 2000 TimeEntry rows fetched in memory for the aggregation. The dashboard calls this on every load.
root_cause: No pagination; inline time-entry aggregation loads all rows to compute totalLoggedHours in JS.
impact: On the dashboard page this runs on every page load for every user. A power user with hundreds of tasks causes a full table scan of task_assignees + tasks + time_entries on each visit.
suggested_fix: Add a take limit (e.g. 200) and use a groupBy aggregation on time_entries server-side instead of loading all rows. Or split into active tasks (PENDING/IN_PROGRESS) and a separate history endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: getTasksByAssignee returns at most 200 tasks
totalLoggedHours is computed via a DB aggregate not JS reduce over hydrated rows

title: findOrphans() has no pagination or limit — returns all orphan tasks
description: Returns every orphan task (projectId IS NULL) with no limit. This set grows unboundedly as recurring meetings, cross-cutting tasks, and so on accumulate. The include brings assignees and their user profiles, plus three _count subqueries. There is no pagination parameter exposed.
root_cause: The method was never given a pagination interface; the design assumes orphan tasks are few.
impact: An organisation that creates many meeting/cross-cutting tasks will accumulate hundreds of orphan tasks. The endpoint becomes progressively slower and the payload grows without bound.
suggested_fix: Add pagination (page/limit) with a default of 20 and a ceiling of 200.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks/orphans returns paginated results with a meta object
Hard cap of 200 per page enforced

title: getMyDoneUndeclaredTasks() is unbounded — DONE tasks without time entries grow indefinitely
description: Fetches every DONE task assigned to the user that has no user time entry. There is no limit. The correlated NOT EXISTS subquery on time_entries runs on every row in the tasks table that matches the assignee filter. This is called on every dashboard load for each user. Old users with many archived DONE tasks get progressively slower results.
root_cause: No pagination; the anti-join subquery (NOT: timeEntries.some) is correlated and forces a row-by-row evaluation.
impact: Dashboard load time scales with historical task count. With 1000 historical DONE tasks and no time entries declared, this scans the full set every visit.
suggested_fix: Add a hard cap (take: 50) since the widget only displays a few items. Consider adding a dedicated dismissal flag to avoid the expensive NOT EXISTS correlated subquery.
acceptance_criteria: getMyDoneUndeclaredTasks returns at most 50 tasks
Dashboard load is not blocked by historical DONE task accumulation

title: importTasks: sequential await inside for-loop — O(N) DB round-trips for N tasks
description: The import loop fires at least 2 sequential DB round-trips per task (findFirst + create) and an additional round-trip per subtask. For a 100-row CSV with 5 subtasks per task this is 600+ sequential DB calls. These are not batched and cannot be parallelised with Promise.all because each row's create depends on the duplicate check result. The outer task creation loop compounds with the inner subtask loop.
root_cause: Row-by-row processing with individual findFirst + create calls; subtasks are also created one-by-one inside a nested loop.
impact: A 100-task CSV import takes 600+ sequential DB round-trips (est. 600ms–6s on a local network, 6–60s on a remote DB). Import UX will time out for large files.
suggested_fix: 1) Batch-fetch all existing titles in one query before the loop (already done for usersByEmail, apply same pattern for titles). 2) Batch-create tasks using createMany. 3) Batch-create subtasks using a single createMany after all task IDs are collected.
acceptance_criteria: importTasks issues at most O(1) DB queries regardless of CSV row count
A 100-row CSV import completes in under 500ms
notes: validateImport does pre-fetch existing titles correctly (existingTasks set) — the same pattern should be applied to importTasks.

title: importUsers: sequential await per user inside for-loop — O(N) DB round-trips
description: The user import loop makes at least 3 sequential awaits per row: findFirst (duplicate check), bcrypt.hash (CPU-bound, ~100ms at cost 12), and user.create. Plus a conditional userService.createMany. For a 50-user CSV import this means 150+ DB/CPU operations run sequentially. bcrypt.hash at cost 12 takes ~100ms per call on modern hardware, so 50 users = 5 seconds of hashing alone.
root_cause: Each row processed individually; duplicate check and bcrypt are both blocking the loop.
impact: A 50-user CSV import takes ~5–10s just for bcrypt hashing, plus DB round-trips. The HTTP request will time out on large imports.
suggested_fix: Pre-fetch all existing emails/logins in one query (as done in validateImport). Run bcrypt.hash calls in parallel with Promise.all for a batch, then createMany. Cap batch size at 20 to avoid overwhelming the event loop.
acceptance_criteria: A 50-user import completes in under 2s
Duplicate check uses a pre-fetched Set not per-row findFirst
notes: validateImport correctly pre-fetches existingUsers in one query — importUsers should mirror that pattern.

title: checkCircularDependency uses a BFS with one DB query per visited node — O(E) round-trips
description: The cycle-detection BFS issues one taskDependency.findMany per BFS node. For a project with a dependency chain of depth 10 this fires 10 sequential DB round-trips. In the worst case (complete DAG of N nodes), this is O(N) sequential round-trips. Since there is no depth limit, a deep dependency graph causes multi-second latency on addDependency calls.
root_cause: BFS implemented with one async DB call per level/node rather than a recursive CTE or a full graph fetch.
impact: addDependency on a project with a deep dependency chain causes latency proportional to chain depth. At depth 20 this is 20 sequential queries (~200ms on a local DB, potentially 2s on a remote DB).
suggested_fix: Replace with a single Postgres recursive CTE query: `WITH RECURSIVE deps AS (SELECT depends_on_task_id FROM task_dependencies WHERE task_id = $1 UNION ALL ...) SELECT * FROM deps WHERE depends_on_task_id = $2 LIMIT 1`. This runs in one round-trip regardless of graph depth.
acceptance_criteria: checkCircularDependency issues exactly 1 DB query regardless of dependency chain depth
A chain of 50 dependencies is checked in under 10ms

title: analytics.getTasks fetches all task rows with no limit — unbounded full-table scan
description: This method returns the full Task[] (all columns) with no limit for the entire analytics scope. The result is then used in calculateMetrics (filter operations in JS) and getProjectDetails (groupBy by projectId in JS). The duplicate groupBy query in getAnalytics (taskStatusGroupBy, line 114) already computes the counts needed; getTasks exists to feed calculateMetrics but only uses status and endDate fields, fetching all task columns is wasteful.
root_cause: getTasks was written to return full Task objects for all downstream consumers; it was not updated when the groupBy path was added.
impact: Analytics for an admin with 50 projects and 2000 tasks fetches 2000 full Task rows (including description, dates, and so on) into Node memory, then filters them in JS. This is redundant because taskStatusGroupBy already has all needed count data.
