suggested_fix: Replace getTasks entirely with the taskStatusGroupBy result. Update calculateMetrics to consume aggregated counts. Remove the full-table task scan from the analytics hot path.
acceptance_criteria: getAnalytics issues zero calls to task.findMany after this fix
calculateMetrics consumes only the groupBy result, not a full Task[]
notes: taskStatusGroupBy on line 114 is redundant with the groupBy inside getProjects on line 189. There are now TWO groupBy queries on task per analytics call.

title: analytics.getAnalytics fires TWO identical task.groupBy queries per request
description: getAnalytics fires taskStatusGroupBy (line 113) over the projectIds from getProjects. But getProjects itself (line 189) also fires an identical task.groupBy by ['projectId','status'] for progress computation. That means every analytics request runs the same GROUP BY on the tasks table twice. Both queries scan the same rows with the same filter.
root_cause: getProjects was refactored to compute progress via groupBy without removing or sharing the outer groupBy that was added later.
impact: Every analytics page load fires one redundant DB query that scans all task rows in scope. On a tenant with 50 projects and 2000 tasks this is a wasted full-table-equivalent group scan.
suggested_fix: Pass the result of the outer taskStatusGroupBy (line 113) into getProjects as a parameter, eliminating the inner groupBy on line 189. Or compute the per-project progress map in getAnalytics and pass it through.
acceptance_criteria: getAnalytics issues exactly ONE task.groupBy per request
Both getProjectProgressData and per-project progress in getProjects use the same pre-computed map

title: analytics members include: loads ALL project members with full user object — no per-project cap
description: getProjects in analytics includes ALL ProjectMember rows for each project, each with a full User object and their Department. Unlike the main project list (which uses take: 5 for members), the analytics query has no limit on members. A project with 50 members multiplied by 50 projects generates 2500 full user rows fetched per analytics call.
root_cause: No take limit on members include in the analytics getProjects path.
impact: Analytics for tenants with many project members causes a combinatorial explosion of hydrated rows. A 50-project tenant with 50 members per project fetches 2500 user+department rows on each analytics page load (60s TTL cache partially mitigates this).
suggested_fix: Add `take: 5` on members include as done in projects.findAll, or better, remove the members include from analytics entirely since the member count is available via _count.members.
acceptance_criteria: analytics members include has a take: 5 cap or is removed
Member user data is not fetched for each of the 50 analytics projects

title: Analytics cache has no mutation invalidation — stale data served for up to 60s after changes
code_evidence:
/** TTL for analytics cache entries (seconds). Balances freshness vs. Prisma load. */
const ANALYTICS_CACHE_TTL = 60;
description: The analytics cache uses TTL-only eviction (60 seconds) with no mutation-bust hooks. When a task is created, completed, or a project status changes, the analytics page continues to show stale data for up to 60 seconds. The CacheService comment acknowledges this: 'TTL-only eviction (no mutation-bust hooks) within this initial bounded scope (AC#6 forbids touching mutation service paths in this commit).' The AC#6 block may still be active.
root_cause: Cache invalidation was deferred to a future commit (AC#6 constraint) and has not been implemented.
impact: Users who change a task status see their analytics unchanged for up to 60 seconds. In a real-time dashboard context this degrades trust in the data. For high-write scenarios (task status transitions during a sprint), the cache hit rate will be high but the data will frequently be stale.
suggested_fix: Add cache invalidation calls in TasksService.update (status change), ProjectsService.update/archive/unarchive, and LeavesService.approve. Use pattern-delete `cache:analytics:${userId}:*` or a dedicated invalidation key per user-scope.
acceptance_criteria: After a task status change, the next analytics request returns fresh data
CacheService.del is called with the analytics key on task/project mutations
notes: The CacheService comment on line 12 explicitly acknowledges this gap.

title: audit_logs advisory lock serialises ALL audit writes globally — creates a bottleneck under concurrent mutations
code_evidence: await tx.$executeRaw`SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('audit_logs_chain'))`;
description: Every audit log write acquires the same global advisory lock on the string 'audit_logs_chain'. This serialises all audit inserts across all concurrent API requests. Under moderate load (10 concurrent requests each triggering an audit write), all 10 requests queue on this lock. Since the lock is held for the duration of a transaction that includes a user lookup + INSERT, hold time can be 5-20ms per write, creating throughput bottleneck of 50-200 audit writes/second.
root_cause: The hash-chained audit log requires strictly ordered inserts to maintain prevHash integrity; the advisory lock is the implementation for this ordering guarantee.
impact: Under concurrent load (e.g. bulk import of 50 users via importUsers), each user creation triggers an audit write. All 50 audit writes queue serially on the advisory lock, adding ~1s of advisory lock wait time to the import. More broadly, any API endpoint that triggers audit writes becomes a contention point.
suggested_fix: Consider whether the strict sequential chain is necessary for ALL audit entries, or only for sensitive actions (ROLE_CHANGE, USER_DELETED, and so on). For high-frequency routine events, use a background queue (Redis list + consumer) that batches inserts with a single advisory lock acquisition. Alternatively, use a monotonic sequence column and accept eventual chain consistency.
acceptance_criteria: Bulk import of 50 users completes without measurable advisory lock wait accumulation
Advisory lock contention is documented as a known tradeoff if not fixed
notes: This is an architectural tradeoff — the hash chain's correctness depends on serialisation. The issue is the scale impact, not the design intent.

title: planning.getOverview loads up to 1000 users and 1000 leaves in a single blocking request
description: The planning overview endpoint hard-codes limit=1000 for both users (allowFullScan: true bypasses the 200 hard ceiling) and services. Combined with leavesService.findAll(1, 1000, ...) in the second parallel batch (line 96-105), this single endpoint loads up to 1000 users + 1000 services + up to 500 tasks + up to 1000 leaves in one request. The response JSON for a mid-sized organisation (200 users, 50 services, 300 leaves) will easily exceed 1 MB.
root_cause: The planning view was designed to render a full grid of all users/leaves/tasks for a date range; the 1000 limits were set as 'practical' ceilings but are effectively unbounded for small-to-medium organisations.
impact: Planning page load for a 500-user organisation triggers 3000+ row hydrations in a single request. Response latency and payload size degrade the planning page UX significantly. The Node.js process must serialise several MB of JSON per request.
suggested_fix: Implement pagination or date-windowed loading for the planning view. Load users and services once (cached), then page-load leaves/tasks. Alternatively, move to a per-service or per-department view that scopes the load.
acceptance_criteria: Planning overview does not load more than 200 users in a single request
Leaves and tasks are loaded for a maximum 4-week window by default

title: projects.getProjectStats fetches full members list (no select) — pulls all member columns
description: In getProjectStats, `members: true` includes every column of every ProjectMember row (id, projectId, userId, role, allocation, startDate, endDate, createdAt) for every member. The method only uses `project.members.length` to compute totalMembers. All other member data is fetched but discarded.
root_cause: members: true was written for convenience; only the count is needed.
impact: For a project with 100 members, this fetches 100 full ProjectMember rows when only the count is needed. The fix is trivial but worth flagging as the stats endpoint is called from the project detail page.
suggested_fix: Replace `members: true` with `_count: { select: { members: true } }` and update the return path to read `project._count.members`.
acceptance_criteria: getProjectStats no longer includes the full members array
_count.members is used for the totalMembers field

title: getSnapshots has no pagination or limit — returns all historical snapshots for a project
description: Returns all ProjectSnapshot rows for a project with no limit. For a project running for 2 years with daily snapshots, this is 730 rows. Without a date filter (from/to are optional), all snapshots for the project's lifetime are returned in a single query.
root_cause: No pagination or default date window; both from and to are optional with no fallback.
impact: A long-running project's snapshot history grows at 1 row/day indefinitely. Fetching all snapshots for a chart without a date filter returns the full history on every request.
suggested_fix: Add a default date window (e.g. last 90 days) when from/to are not provided, and add a hard limit of 365 rows.
acceptance_criteria: getSnapshots with no date filter returns at most the last 90 days
A hard take limit of 365 is enforced

title: getProjectStats issues 2 separate TimeEntry findMany — should use one groupBy or aggregate
description: getProjectStats fetches all user time entries and all third-party time entries for a project's task IDs, then reduces them in JavaScript. For a project with 100 tasks and 500 time entries, this fetches 500 rows just for the hours column. The aggregation should be pushed to Postgres via aggregate()._sum.hours.
root_cause: findMany used where aggregate is appropriate; JS reduce replaces a DB SUM.
impact: A project with 1000 time entries fetches 1000 rows to compute two SUM values. Using aggregate() would return the same result in a single row.
suggested_fix: Replace both findMany calls with `this.prisma.timeEntry.aggregate({ _sum: { hours: true }, where: {...} })` for each dimension.
acceptance_criteria: getProjectStats uses timeEntry.aggregate not findMany for hour totals
No JS reduce over time entry arrays in getProjectStats

title: captureSnapshots loads all ACTIVE project tasks in memory — no streaming or groupBy
description: captureSnapshots loads all ACTIVE projects with their full task status arrays and milestone arrays into Node memory. For 100 active projects each with 200 tasks, this is 20,000 task status rows loaded into memory. The status counts are then computed with JS .filter().length — three passes per project. This runs on a scheduled timer tick.
root_cause: The comment mentions 'PER-003: batched — 2 DB queries' but the first query still loads all task rows (select: { status: true }) into Node memory for JS aggregation instead of using a groupBy.
impact: With 100 active projects and 200 tasks each, the snapshot scheduler loads 20,000 task rows every time it runs. A Prisma groupBy would return exactly N(projects)×N(statuses) = ~500 rows.
suggested_fix: Replace the nested tasks select with a groupBy on task by ['projectId', 'status'] over all active project IDs. Same for milestones if volume warrants it.
acceptance_criteria: captureSnapshots uses task.groupBy not task select inside project include
Node memory footprint of captureSnapshots is O(P×S) not O(P×T) where S=status count (5), T=task count

title: TaskDependency table has no index on dependsOnTaskId — BFS cycle check and DELETE are sequential scans
code_evidence:
model TaskDependency {
  id              String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId          String
  dependsOnTaskId String
  createdAt       DateTime @default(now())
  ...
  @@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId])
  @@map("task_dependencies")
}
description: TaskDependency has a composite unique on (taskId, dependsOnTaskId) which creates an index usable for lookups by taskId. However, there is no index on dependsOnTaskId alone. The reverse lookup `WHERE dependsOnTaskId = X` (used by the `dependents` relation in remove() to check if any task depends on the one being deleted) and the cycle-check BFS `WHERE taskId = currentTaskId` both scan the table. The dependents check on Task.remove fires `include: { dependents: true }` which generates WHERE task_id = X — covered. But the BFS check WHERE taskId = currentTaskId is also covered. The reverse WHERE depends_on_task_id = X for the `dependents` relation on Task (used in findOne) is NOT covered by the leading edge of the unique index.
root_cause: The unique index is composite (taskId, dependsOnTaskId). Postgres can use it for taskId lookups but NOT for dependsOnTaskId-only lookups. The DependentTasks relation query uses dependsOnTaskId as the FK which scans the table.
impact: Lookups on task.dependents (used in findOne, remove) require a sequential scan of task_dependencies when the table grows large. For a project with 1000 dependency edges, each findOne triggers a sequential scan.
suggested_fix: Add `@@index([dependsOnTaskId])` to TaskDependency in schema.prisma and run a migration.
acceptance_criteria: schema.prisma has @@index([dependsOnTaskId]) on TaskDependency
EXPLAIN ANALYZE for dependents lookup shows Index Scan not Seq Scan

title: TaskRACI has no index on userId — user-scoped RACI lookups scan the full table
code_evidence:
model TaskRACI {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId    String
  userId    String
  role      RACIRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  ...
  @@unique([taskId, userId, role])
  @@map("task_raci")
}
description: TaskRACI has a unique on (taskId, userId, role). The unique index can satisfy lookups by taskId but NOT by userId alone. Any query filtering by userId (e.g. 'what tasks is user X responsible for?') would require a sequential scan. Additionally the unique index leading on taskId means the userId position in the composite is not independently indexed.
root_cause: No secondary index on userId for the TaskRACI model.
impact: Future user-scoped RACI queries (if added) will scan the full table. Currently the raci relation in findOne filters by taskId (covered), so the immediate impact is limited. However it is a correctness gap for future queries.
suggested_fix: Add `@@index([userId])` to TaskRACI in schema.prisma.
acceptance_criteria: schema.prisma has @@index([userId]) on TaskRACI
Query plan for userId-scoped RACI lookup shows Index Scan
notes: Severity is important rather than blocking because current query paths filter by taskId (covered). This becomes blocking if user-scoped RACI views are added.

title: comments.findAll default limit is 1000 — same ceiling as a de facto unbounded list
description: Comments findAll uses the same default=1000 / ceiling=1000 pattern as projects. A task with thousands of comments (unlikely but possible for a long-lived task) would return all comments in one request. The include of author and task adds additional joins per comment row.
root_cause: Default limit set to 1000 matching the ceiling, effectively paginating in a single page.
impact: Low probability issue given comment volumes, but the pattern is inconsistent with good practice. Comment lists are typically rendered in-page and don't need more than 20-50 per view.
suggested_fix: Lower default to 20, ceiling to 200.
acceptance_criteria: comments.findAll default limit is 20 or less
ceiling does not exceed 200

title: All app pages are 'use client' — no RSC, no TanStack Query, no incremental hydration
description: Every page in the App Router (dashboard, projects, tasks, leaves, and so on) is marked 'use client' and uses useEffect + useState for data fetching. This means the full JS bundle is shipped to the client on every navigation, no static or server-side rendering is used, and the page is blank until the client-side useEffect fires and the API response returns. TanStack Query is installed (in package.json via QueryProvider) but is not used in any page component for data fetching — all pages use useEffect + useState + direct service calls.
root_cause: The frontend was built in a client-first pattern without adopting Server Components or TanStack Query's cache. The App Router's streaming/RSC capability is unused.
impact: Every page load ships the full React component tree + all hooks to the client. There is no server-side data fetching, no stale-while-revalidate caching, and no background refetching. Users on slow connections see blank pages until multiple sequential API calls complete in useEffect.
suggested_fix: Migrate data-fetching pages to use TanStack Query's useQuery hooks (already installed) for automatic caching, background refetch, and loading states. For static content (user list, departments), use RSC with server-side Prisma calls. At minimum, wrap the main data fetches in useQuery instead of useEffect+setState.
acceptance_criteria: At least one high-traffic page (dashboard or projects) uses useQuery for data fetching
No page uses useEffect+setState for primary data fetching when TanStack Query is available
verification_command: grep -rn 'useQuery\|useMutation' apps/web/src/ apps/web/app/ | grep -v node_modules | wc -l
notes: QueryProvider is correctly set up (apps/web/src/components/QueryProvider.tsx) but appears unused by actual page components.

title: fetchManagersAndDepartments called on every Create button click — not cached between invocations
description: Every click of the 'Create Project' button re-fetches the full user list and all departments, even if the modal was opened and closed moments before. These are reference data that change infrequently. The refetch costs 2 sequential network round-trips per modal open.
root_cause: No caching of reference data; fetched on demand per modal open.
impact: Minor UX degradation (slight delay before manager dropdown is populated on modal open). Not a critical performance issue but a common anti-pattern.
suggested_fix: Fetch managers and departments once on page mount and cache in state, or use TanStack Query with a long staleTime (5 minutes) for reference data.
acceptance_criteria: Opening the Create Project modal does not fire new API requests if data was fetched in the last 5 minutes

title: AuditLog has no index on createdAt+id — ORDER BY createdAt DESC, id DESC scans the full table
description: The prevHash lookup inside every audit write sorts by (createdAt DESC, id DESC) LIMIT 1. Without a composite index on (createdAt, id), Postgres must sort the full audit_logs table to find the last row. As the audit table grows (the hash chain is immutable so rows are never deleted), this sort cost increases with table size.
root_cause: No composite index on (createdAt DESC, id DESC) on audit_logs.
impact: Every audit write fires a full table sort on audit_logs to find the prevHash. With 1 million audit rows this is a multi-second operation. The advisory lock holds during this sort, making the bottleneck compound (PERF-014 + PERF-025).
suggested_fix: Add `@@index([createdAt(sort: Desc), id(sort: Desc)])` to the AuditLog model in schema.prisma. Verify with EXPLAIN ANALYZE.
acceptance_criteria: schema.prisma has a composite index on AuditLog(createdAt DESC, id DESC)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE for the prevHash query shows Index Scan not Sort+Seq Scan
notes: Prisma may not support sort-direction indexes in schema.prisma PSL — a raw SQL migration may be required.

title: UsersService.create() emits no audit row when admin creates a user
description: The create() method (lines 88–219) persists a new User row and creates UserService memberships, but emits neither an AuditService.log() call nor an AuditPersistenceService.log() call. Every other sensitive user lifecycle mutation (update, remove, hardDelete, changePassword, resetPassword) emits at least one audit row. The REGISTER action (auth.service.ts:362) only covers self-registration; the admin-create path through POST /users is silent. An auditor cannot reconstruct who created a given account.
root_cause: create() in UsersService was never wired to AuditService/AuditPersistenceService despite the AuditService being injected in the constructor.
impact: No audit trail for admin-created accounts; Cour des Comptes traceability gap for all account provisioning done via POST /users.
suggested_fix: After the successful user.create() at line 153, emit: `await this.auditPersistence.log({ action: AuditAction.REGISTER, entityType: 'User', entityId: user.id, actorId: caller?.id ?? null, payload: { before: null, after: { roleCode: user.role?.code, isActive: user.isActive } } })` — or add a dedicated USER_CREATED action following the same pattern as USER_DEACTIVATED.
acceptance_criteria: POST /users by an ADMIN produces an audit_logs row with a recognized action (REGISTER or a new USER_CREATED) within 1s
The row carries actorId of the creating admin and entityId of the new user
The AuditAction enum and payload-schemas.ts are updated if a new action is introduced
verification_command: curl -s -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/users -H 'Authorization: Bearer <admin-jwt>' -d '{...}' && docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U orchestr_a -d orchestr_a_prod -c "SELECT action, entityid, actorid FROM audit_logs ORDER BY createdat DESC LIMIT 3;"
notes: auditService and auditPersistence are both injected (lines 57-58) but never called in create().

title: UsersService.importUsers() creates N users with no audit row per account
description: importUsers() bulk-creates users row by row but emits no AuditPersistenceService.log() per created account. A batch of 100 account creations leaves no individual audit rows. The REGISTER action schema already accommodates this path; the per-row loop at lines 1301-1445 is the correct place to emit. Unlike create(), importUsers() has no callerRoleCode parameter for actorId — the controller passes it (line 122) but no caller User object is threaded. An admin importing 50 accounts is completely invisible in the audit trail.
root_cause: importUsers() was not updated when AUD-EMIT-001 / OBS-004 wired audit emissions into the other user mutation methods.
impact: Bulk account provisioning (POST /users/import) leaves no audit trail; an admin can create arbitrary accounts invisibly.
suggested_fix: Resolve caller to a User entity in the controller (or add callerUserId parameter), then emit REGISTER per created user inside the loop, mirroring auth.service.ts:362. A single SYSTEM_BACKFILL-style batch summary row is an acceptable alternative if per-row volume is a concern.
acceptance_criteria: POST /users/import by ADMIN with N valid rows creates at least N audit_logs rows
Each row carries actorId of the importing admin

title: ACCESS_DENIED action is defined and schema'd but never emitted by any guard or filter
description: AuditAction.ACCESS_DENIED is declared in audit-action.enum.ts:27, has an entity-type mapping in audit.service.ts:56, and a payload schema (payload-schemas.ts:160). However grep across all non-spec source shows zero AuditAction.ACCESS_DENIED call sites. The PermissionsGuardV2 logs a structured warn() to stdout but emits no persistent audit row. AllExceptionsFilter (line 21-25) explicitly defers this. The stdout log line has no userId, no targetedResource, no IP.
root_cause: AllExceptionsFilter and PermissionsGuardV2 both have documented deferrals for ACCESS_DENIED audit emission; neither was followed up with an implementation.
impact: Every RBAC-denied request (403) is invisible in the audit_logs hash-chain; an attacker probing privilege-escalation paths leaves no durable forensic trace.
suggested_fix: In PermissionsGuardV2.canActivate(), when returning false on a permission failure, fire-and-forget emit AuditAction.ACCESS_DENIED with the userId from request.user, the route, and the missing permission. The guard already has the ExecutionContext and can read request.id for correlation.
acceptance_criteria: A 403 response from PermissionsGuardV2 produces an ACCESS_DENIED audit_logs row within 1s
The row payload contains the denied userId and the route identifier
notes: The JwtAuthGuard path (unauthenticated 401) is a separate concern; only the post-auth 403 RBAC-denial path is flagged here.

title: DOCUMENT_DOWNLOADED action is defined but wired to no emitter (binary egress unobservable)
description: AuditAction.DOCUMENT_DOWNLOADED is in the enum, has an entity-type entry (audit.service.ts:71), and a payload schema (payload-schemas.ts), but no code emits it. The comment explains the bypass: Document.url points to external storage, so actual byte transfer bypasses the API. The gap is documented but the risk is real: an auditor querying DOCUMENT_DOWNLOADED for RGPD personal-data egress will find zero rows even if documents were accessed. DOCUMENT_READ only covers metadata fetch, not byte download.
root_cause: Documents are metadata-only in the current architecture; the storage backend transfers bytes without going through the NestJS layer, so the DOWNLOADED event cannot be emitted server-side without a proxy or presigned-URL log hook.
impact: RGPD audit coverage for document byte-level egress is zero; no forensic trace of who downloaded which file. Relevant if the document store is ever migrated to a proxied download path.
suggested_fix: Either (a) implement a proxied download endpoint that streams from external storage through the API and emits DOCUMENT_DOWNLOADED, or (b) document the deliberate gap in a RGPD treatment record and add a compliance warning to the OBS-006 inline comment. If option (b), add a TODO with a tracking ticket ID so the gap is not forgotten.
acceptance_criteria: If a proxied download endpoint exists: it emits DOCUMENT_DOWNLOADED per successful download
If no proxied path: a RGPD treatment record or code comment explicitly acknowledges the gap with remediation criteria
notes: The enum comment (lines 49-53) is the primary source; the gap is explicitly acknowledged but not mitigated.

title: getRequestId() has zero call sites: ALS request-ID never reaches audit payloads or structured logs
description: OBS-009 wired AsyncLocalStorage to capture every request's ID (main.ts:110). The payload-schemas.ts securityEnvelope schema declares an optional `requestId` field (line 70) so audit rows can carry correlation IDs. However, grep across all non-spec, non-definition source files finds zero call sites for getRequestId(). AuditService.log() never reads it; AuditPersistenceService never passes it through. AllExceptionsFilter reads request.id directly from the Fastify object (line 77) but does not propagate it into the NestJS Logger or to downstream audit rows. The ALS store exists, the accessor exists, the schema slot exists — but nothing plumbs them together.
root_cause: getRequestId() was defined as part of OBS-009 but was never invoked in the audit emission or logging code paths.
impact: In production, it is impossible to stitch a specific audit_logs row back to its originating HTTP request via log correlation; incident investigation requires time-window matching rather than direct ID lookup.
suggested_fix: In AuditService.log(), read `getRequestId()` from the context and add it to the entry object passed to this.logger.log(). In the security-envelope path, also forward it as `requestId` in the AuditPersistenceService payload. A single import of getRequestId into audit.service.ts and a one-line spread into the payload is sufficient.
acceptance_criteria: A login request with X-Request-ID: test-id-123 produces an audit_logs row whose payload.requestId === 'test-id-123'
The server-side NestJS Logger line for the same event contains the request ID
notes: payload-schemas.ts:57-70 confirms the schema slot is prepared; the gap is purely in the call-site wiring.

title: generateResetToken() emits PASSWORD_CHANGED for a token-generation event, not a password change
description: generateResetToken() (auth.service.ts:479) issues a cryptographic reset token — it does NOT change the password. Yet it emits AuditAction.PASSWORD_CHANGED, the same action used by actual password changes (changePassword() and resetPassword()). An auditor querying PASSWORD_CHANGED rows to understand password-change history will find token-generation events mixed in. The correct action would be PASSWORD_RESET_BY_ADMIN (which UsersService.resetPassword() already uses for admin-driven changes) or a dedicated PASSWORD_RESET_TOKEN_ISSUED action.
root_cause: No dedicated audit action for token issuance; PASSWORD_CHANGED was the closest available action when the code was written.
impact: Audit log conflates token issuance with actual password mutations, making it impossible to distinguish 'password was changed' from 'a reset link was sent' by action code alone; forensic queries return false positives.
suggested_fix: Either add AuditAction.PASSWORD_RESET_TOKEN_ISSUED to the enum (with a securityEnvelope schema) and update the call in generateResetToken(), or reuse AuditAction.PASSWORD_RESET_BY_ADMIN with a payload field `phase: 'TOKEN_ISSUED'` to distinguish it from `phase: 'PASSWORD_SET'`.
acceptance_criteria: A call to generateResetToken() produces an audit_logs row with an action distinct from PASSWORD_CHANGED
Querying audit_logs WHERE action='PASSWORD_CHANGED' does NOT return token-issuance rows
notes: auth.service.ts:587 (resetPassword after token use) correctly uses PASSWORD_CHANGED; only the generateResetToken path at line 524 is misclassified.

title: Sentry/error-tracking is a no-op stub in both AllExceptionsFilter and error-reporter; unhandled exceptions are logged locally only
description: AllExceptionsFilter.catch() calls SentryClient.captureException() at line 91, but SentryClient is a local no-op constant that never contacts any remote service. Similarly, NoopErrorReporter in error-reporter.ts logs unhandled rejections/uncaughtExceptions to the NestJS Logger only. The .env.production.example has SENTRY_DSN commented out (line 103) but the application reads no SENTRY_DSN variable — even if the operator sets it, nothing forwards errors to Sentry. The ErrorReporter interface is a well-designed extension point, but the extension is never wired.
root_cause: The Sentry dependency was intentionally deferred; the no-op stubs were the design intent but the wiring step was never completed.
impact: Unhandled 500 errors and process-level rejections are only visible in container stdout/json-file logs. On the VPS (json-file driver, 50MB/5-file rotation) these logs are ephemeral; a silent crash may go undetected unless someone is actively tailing logs.
suggested_fix: Either (a) install @sentry/node and wire it in error-reporter.ts (reading process.env.SENTRY_DSN), or (b) use an open-source RGPD-compatible alternative (GlitchTip, self-hosted Sentry). Add SENTRY_DSN to .env.production.example and docker-compose.prod.yml env passthrough. The ErrorReporter interface and installGlobalErrorHandlers are already the correct extension points.
acceptance_criteria: When SENTRY_DSN is set, an unhandled exception or uncaughtRejection causes a network call to the configured DSN within 5s
SENTRY_DSN appears as a documented optional variable in .env.production.example
notes: The SENTRY_DSN passthrough is also missing from docker-compose.prod.yml environment block.

title: In-memory Prometheus metrics are fully reset on every container restart
description: MetricsService stores http_requests_total and http_request_duration_seconds in two in-process Maps. On every container restart, rolling update, or OOM kill, all accumulated metric state is lost. For a production system on docker-compose.prod.yml (restart: unless-stopped, max_attempts: 3), the container restarts at least once per deploy. Prometheus scrape intervals between restarts collect counter values that reset to 0, making rate() and increase() calculations incorrect. There is no persistence layer (Redis, remote-write, or prom-client with pushgateway).
root_cause: The metrics service was designed as a minimal scaffold (OBS-011) with no persistence; in-process Maps were the simplest approach.
impact: Counter resets mislead SRE dashboards; a rate() alert on http_requests_total will see a spike-to-zero on every deploy and may either false-alarm or miss real traffic loss. Duration summaries also reset, losing SLA percentile history.
suggested_fix: Replace the in-process Maps with prom-client (the de-facto Node.js Prometheus library) and configure a Prometheus scrape interval short enough to catch data before restart, or add a remote_write endpoint. Alternatively, expose metrics via a push-on-shutdown hook to a Pushgateway. Minimum: document the restart-reset behavior as a known limitation on the /api/metrics endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: http_requests_total counter value is not reset to 0 on container restart
Or: the /api/metrics response includes a last_reset_at metric so downstream tooling can detect and compensate

title: No database connection-pool metrics or Redis latency metrics exposed at /api/metrics
code_evidence:
/**
 * OBS-011 — Minimal in-process Prometheus metrics service.
 *
 * Tracks http_requests_total (counter) and http_request_duration_seconds (summary).
 * No external dependency (no prom-client). Output is valid Prometheus text format.
 */
description: The OBS-011 Prometheus endpoint tracks only HTTP request counts and durations. There are no metrics for: Prisma connection pool saturation (active/idle connections), PostgreSQL query error rates, Redis PING latency or connection failures, audit chain advisory lock wait time, or background snapshot-scheduler job timing. PrismaService.onModuleInit() wires a slow-query log listener (OBS-023) but this is stdout-only and not exported to Prometheus. A DB connection-pool exhaustion or Redis outage would be visible only in container logs, not in alerting dashboards.
root_cause: OBS-011 explicitly documented itself as 'minimal'; no follow-up ticket wired DB/Redis instrumentation.
impact: An operator cannot alert on DB pool exhaustion, Redis latency, or audit-chain lock contention without manual log grep. Incident MTTD (mean time to detect) for database-layer degradation is unbounded.
suggested_fix: Add Prometheus gauges/histograms for: Prisma $metrics (available via prisma.$metrics.json()), Redis PING latency (measured in the RedisService on each operation), and audit advisory lock wait time (measured in AuditPersistenceService.log()). These can be pushed to MetricsService.recordRequest() equivalent or a new recordGauge() method.
acceptance_criteria: GET /api/metrics includes at least one metric covering DB connection state
GET /api/metrics includes at least one metric covering Redis latency

title: console.error() in UsersService.create() bypasses NestJS Logger and Fastify log redaction
description: UsersService.create() calls console.error() directly at line 147. This bypasses the NestJS Logger (which routes through Fastify's pino logger with structured fields and log-level filtering), and critically bypasses the Fastify redact.config.ts redaction layer. The interpolated value is createUserDto.login — a user-supplied login identifier that could be an email address (PII). Although the message is a critical internal error, the redaction pipeline configured in fastifyLoggerOptions never sees it. All other business-layer log emissions use this.logger (NestJS Logger).
root_cause: Quick defensive log added without checking the Logger pattern.
impact: If createUserDto.login is an email address, it lands in unredacted stdout/container logs, violating the PII-scrubbing contract established by redact.config.ts.
suggested_fix: Replace console.error() with `this.logger.error('[CRITICAL] bcrypt hash verification failed for user ' + createUserDto.login)` — or, since login is PII, log only the userId placeholder before the user is created: `this.logger.error('[CRITICAL] bcrypt hash verification failed (login redacted)')` and rely on the thrown Error to surface the context.
acceptance_criteria: No console.* calls remain in apps/api/src/*.ts outside scripts/ and witness files
The bcrypt failure log line does not contain the raw login value in production output
verification_command: grep -rn 'console\.' /home/alex/Documents/REPO/ORCHESTRA/apps/api/src --include='*.ts' | grep -v '.spec.ts' | grep -v 'scripts/' | grep -v 'node_modules' | grep -v dist

title: console.warn() in SettingsService.onModuleInit() bypasses NestJS Logger
description: SettingsService.onModuleInit() uses console.warn() instead of this.logger.warn(). This logs outside Fastify's pino pipeline, losing structured fields (service name, log level as machine-readable field, timestamp format consistency) and bypassing LOG_LEVEL filtering. This is a module-init path that runs on every container boot.
root_cause: SettingsService has no private Logger instance injected.
impact: Minor — the settings being non-security-critical means no PII is at risk, but the log line bypasses LOG_LEVEL filtering and appears in stdout regardless of log level configuration.
suggested_fix: Add `private readonly logger = new Logger(SettingsService.name);` to the class and replace console.warn() with this.logger.warn().
acceptance_criteria: No console.* calls remain in SettingsService
The startup warning appears in NestJS structured log format

title: No declared retention policy or partitioning for audit_logs; unbounded table growth
code_evidence:
-- OBS-002 + DAT-009 — audit_logs durability hardening
-- (a) BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger -> RAISE EXCEPTION (immutability)
-- (c) prevHash/rowHash sha256 integrity chain
-- (d) actorEmail/actorLabel snapshot columns
--
-- Ordering is load-bearing: all backfill UPDATEs MUST run BEFORE the
-- immutability trigger is created
description: The audit_logs table is append-only (immutability trigger blocks UPDATE/DELETE) and has no PostgreSQL partitioning, archival cron, or declared retention window. The RGPD treatment record referenced in code comments does not appear to include a data-minimisation timeline. Over time, especially with LEAVE_* events, DOCUMENT_READ events, and LOGIN_SUCCESS events per user per day, the table will grow unboundedly. There is no pg_partman or partitioned-table setup in any migration. The only comment about retention relates to backup tables (migration 20260603120000), not audit_logs itself.
root_cause: OBS-002 focused on integrity/immutability; retention and partitioning were explicitly deferred (no ticket reference found).
impact: At expected event volume (authentication + leave approval + document reads), audit_logs may grow to tens of millions of rows within 2-3 years, degrading query performance on the advisory-lock chain reads. The append-only constraint prevents archival without schema changes.
suggested_fix: Define a retention policy: either (a) pg_partman range partitioning by month on createdAt with archive/detach semantics (rows become read-only in detached partitions, satisfying WORM), or (b) a documented 7-year RGPD retention followed by a controlled purge of non-security-critical action codes. Add the policy as a comment on the audit_logs table and a migration stub.
acceptance_criteria: A documented retention policy exists for audit_logs (in migration comment or RGPD register)
Either: partition by time is implemented, or a purge mechanism exists for non-critical action codes
verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U orchestr_a -d orchestr_a_prod -c "SELECT count(*) FROM audit_logs;"

title: AUDIT_HASH_KEY is absent from .env.production.example and from init-env.sh output
description: main.ts:69 calls assertAuditHashKey(process.env.AUDIT_HASH_KEY) which refuses to boot in any environment without it. docker-compose.prod.yml:127 passes it as a required variable (AUDIT_HASH_KEY: ${AUDIT_HASH_KEY:?AUDIT_HASH_KEY is required}). However, .env.production.example has no AUDIT_HASH_KEY entry, and scripts/init-env.sh does not generate one — it only generates DATABASE_PASSWORD, REDIS_PASSWORD, and JWT_SECRET. An operator following the documented setup path (copy .env.production.example, or run init-env.sh) will produce a file missing this required variable, causing an immediate boot failure with a cryptic error. The .env.example (dev template) does include AUDIT_HASH_KEY=.
root_cause: .env.production.example was not updated when OBS-028 added the AUDIT_HASH_KEY boot assertion.
impact: Operators following the recommended setup path will fail to boot the production container; the error message from assertAuditHashKey will point at the missing key but the fix is non-obvious without reading the source.
suggested_fix: Add `AUDIT_HASH_KEY=` to the SECRETS section of .env.production.example with a comment matching the one in .env.example ('Keys the HMAC that pseudonymises attempted-login identifiers — min 32 chars, stable across deploys'). Add AUDIT_HASH_KEY=$(generate_secret 32) to scripts/init-env.sh between JWT_SECRET and CORS_ORIGIN generation.
acceptance_criteria: .env.production.example contains AUDIT_HASH_KEY in the SECRETS section
scripts/init-env.sh generates and writes AUDIT_HASH_KEY to the output file
notes: docker-compose.prod.yml:127 already has the correct :? guard; the gap is only in the template files used during operator setup.

title: No OpenTelemetry or distributed tracing; requestId correlation stops at the API layer
description: The application propagates a per-request UUID via AsyncLocalStorage (OBS-009) but has no OpenTelemetry SDK, no trace/span IDs, and no W3C TraceContext header propagation. The Next.js web package's required-server-files.json references @effect/opentelemetry but this is a transitive dependency from an Effect library, not an active instrumentation. When the web layer calls the API, requests carry no traceparent header; the nginx proxy adds no trace headers; Prisma queries are not instrumented. Multi-hop debugging (nginx → Next.js proxy → NestJS → PostgreSQL) requires manual correlation across four separate log streams.
root_cause: OBS-009 covered single-service correlation; distributed tracing was not in scope.
impact: Performance profiling and incident root-cause analysis across the full request chain requires manual log correlation; no trace-based tooling (Jaeger, Tempo, and so on) can be integrated without a tracing SDK.
suggested_fix: Install @opentelemetry/sdk-node and configure it in main.ts before NestFactory.create(). Enable the @nestjs/otel-sdk community package or manual instrumentation for Fastify + Prisma. Export traces to a local Jaeger or OTLP collector. This is a suggestion/nit tier given the current single-node architecture.
acceptance_criteria: HTTP requests carry W3C traceparent headers between web and API
Spans are emitted for each NestJS handler invocation and each Prisma query
notes: The @effect/opentelemetry reference in apps/web/.next/required-server-files.json is a transitive Effect library dependency, NOT active tracing instrumentation.

title: PermissionsGuardV2 deny due to missing decorator (uncovered route in enforce mode) logs but does not audit
description: When a route lacks both @RequirePermissions and @AllowSelfService in enforce mode, PermissionsGuardV2 logs a structured warn and returns false (403). The log includes the routeId but not the requesting user's ID or IP. This is a different code path from OBS-003 (which covers routes WITH a permission decorator where the user lacks the permission). Both paths result in 403 but are currently both unaudited. This path additionally risks hiding misconfiguration — a route returning 403 to all users due to a missing decorator looks identical in logs to a legitimate permission denial.
root_cause: The guard has no AuditService injection; the warn() is the extent of observability on this path.
impact: A misconfigured route (missing RBAC decorator) silently returns 403 to all users; the only signal is a NestJS warn log with no user context.
suggested_fix: Inject AuditService into PermissionsGuardV2 and emit ACCESS_DENIED on both the 'no decorator' and 'missing permission' deny paths. Alternatively, add the user ID to the existing warn() log line.
acceptance_criteria: A 403 from a route missing its RBAC decorator includes the requesting userId in the log line
notes: This is distinct from OBS-003 (which covers the permission-check failure path); both paths share the remediation approach.

title: 5 legacy E2E specs use UI login (helpers.ts) instead of API-based storageState
description: e2e/full-workflow.spec.ts, e2e/planning.spec.ts, e2e/leaves.spec.ts, e2e/tasks.spec.ts, e2e/projects.spec.ts all import `login` from `./helpers` and call `await login(page)` which performs a UI form fill (`page.goto('/login')`, fill inputs, click submit). The CLAUDE.md repo convention is explicit: 'Auth via API (never UI login in tests) using storage states in playwright/.auth/'. These specs run under the `chromium` Playwright project (no storageState dependency), meaning they hit the login throttle bucket on every run, are slower, and couple test setup to UI changes.
root_cause: These specs predate the auth.setup.ts/storageState pattern and were never migrated.
impact: Each run of these 5 specs fires real POST /auth/login via the browser UI, consuming the 5/min throttle bucket (SEC-005). In CI with retries this can cause rate-limit 429s contaminating other tests. Login form changes break these tests.
suggested_fix: Migrate each spec to the `e2e/tests/` directory, import from `../../fixtures/test-fixtures` (which provides the `asRole` fixture and storageState), and remove the `login()` call. The `chromium` project in playwright.config.ts has no dependencies — these specs bypass the `setup` project entirely.
acceptance_criteria: No spec under e2e/ (root level) imports from ./helpers
CI run of the 5 converted specs does not call POST /auth/login via UI
Converted specs pass with the admin storageState
notes: The helpers.ts login uses hardcoded credentials admin/admin123. The 5 affected files: e2e/full-workflow.spec.ts, e2e/planning.spec.ts, e2e/leaves.spec.ts, e2e/tasks.spec.ts, e2e/projects.spec.ts.

title: e2e/leaves.spec.ts: tautological assertions — test always passes even when page is broken
description: The assertion `expect(hasList || hasEmptyMessage || isOnLeavesPage).toBeTruthy()` is a tautology: because the test just navigated to `/leaves`, `isOnLeavesPage` is always `true`, making the disjunction always pass regardless of actual content rendering. The same pattern appears at line 208 (`hasPendingLeaves || hasEmptyMessage || isOnLeavesPage`). Additionally, lines 92, 118, 142, 162, 243, 288 use `catch(() => {})` to silently swallow selector failures. If the leaves page renders a blank error screen, all these tests still pass.
root_cause: Defensive coding that trades false-negative safety for no assertion value; these were written to avoid flakes but removed all signal.
impact: A regression that breaks the leaves page entirely (blank render, API error, 500) is invisible to these tests. They will always report green.
suggested_fix: Replace tautological disjunctions with targeted assertions on actual leaf data: use `await expect(page.locator('h1')).toContainText(/congés/i)` and for list presence use `await expect(page.locator('table, [data-testid="leaves-list"]')).toBeVisible()` with a proper timeout. Remove silent `catch(() => {})` blocks and let Playwright report the locator failure.
acceptance_criteria: No `expect(... || isOnLeavesPage).toBeTruthy()` pattern in leaves.spec.ts
At least one assertion fails when the page renders empty (no table, no list)

title: e2e/planning.spec.ts: two `expect(true).toBeTruthy()` assertions — tests can never fail
description: `expect(true).toBeTruthy()` appears at lines 170 and 257 in planning.spec.ts. These are pure no-ops: the test exercises drag-and-drop and 'navigate to today' interactions but makes no assertion about the resulting state. If the interaction silently fails (the task stays in its column, the view does not update), the test passes. Line 199 also has a silent `catch(() => {})` eating selector failures.
root_cause: Placeholder assertions left in place because the author noted the state was hard to verify without proper test IDs on the planning grid.
impact: Drag-and-drop regressions and navigation failures in the planning view are completely invisible to CI.
suggested_fix: Add `data-testid` attributes to planning grid cells/slots in the web component, then assert that the dragged task appears at the new position. For the 'today' navigation, assert that the current week's date header is visible. At minimum, replace `expect(true).toBeTruthy()` with `expect(page.url()).toContain('/planning')` as a floor.
acceptance_criteria: No `expect(true).toBeTruthy()` in planning.spec.ts
Drag-and-drop test asserts task moved to target cell

title: settings.controller.ts has no controller spec (8 endpoints, all @RequirePermissions)
description: SettingsController has 8 endpoints (GET /, GET /category/:category, GET /:key, PUT /:key, POST /bulk, POST /:key/reset, POST /reset-all, DELETE /:key) all decorated with @RequirePermissions. There is a settings.service.spec.ts but no settings.controller.spec.ts. Controller-level tests verify that the controller passes correct arguments to the service, raises correct HTTP exceptions on errors, and that the guard decoration is present. The service spec does not test the controller layer.
root_cause: Controller spec was never written when the settings module was added.
impact: Any regression in controller-level behavior (wrong param passing, missing error translation, wrong HTTP status code) will not be caught by unit tests and must rely on E2E.
suggested_fix: Create apps/api/src/settings/settings.controller.spec.ts following the pattern in tasks.controller.spec.ts: mock SettingsService and PermissionsService, test each endpoint for happy path and at least NotFoundException/ForbiddenException paths.
acceptance_criteria: apps/api/src/settings/settings.controller.spec.ts exists
All 8 endpoints have at least one happy-path test
PUT/:key and DELETE/:key have NotFoundException tests

title: e2e/tasks.spec.ts: task-list test swallows selector failure and asserts only URL
description: The waitForSelector failure is silently caught and the only assertion is `expect(page.url()).toContain('/tasks')`. The URL is set by page.goto() regardless of render outcome, so this assertion is always true. If the tasks page crashes and shows a blank error component, this test passes. This test provides no signal about task list rendering.
root_cause: Defensive catch block and URL-only assertion provide no actual verification of content.
impact: Task list regressions (API failure, empty render, component crash) are invisible.
suggested_fix: Replace with `await expect(page.locator('h1')).toContainText(/tâches/i)` after page.goto. If the list may be empty, assert `await expect(page.locator('h1, [data-testid="empty-state"]').first()).toBeVisible()`.
acceptance_criteria: Test asserts task page H1 is visible
No catch(() => {}) on the selector wait

title: e2e/tests/reports/analytics-advanced.spec.ts: no RBAC denial test for contributeur/observateur
description: The analytics-advanced E2E spec tests only the happy path (admin/manager can view the tab) and includes no denial assertions for roles lacking reports:view (contributeur, observateur). The permission-matrix covers reports:view and reports:export at the API level via api-permissions.spec.ts, but the analytics-advanced E2E only verifies the UI renders correctly — it does not assert that unauthorized roles are redirected or see an error. The spec imports from @playwright/test directly (not test-fixtures) but relies on storageState from the parent project.
root_cause: Spec was written for happy-path smoke, RBAC denial was delegated entirely to api-permissions.spec.ts.
impact: Low — the API-level RBAC is covered by api-permissions.spec.ts. But a frontend bug that renders the reports tab to all roles regardless of API 403 would be undetected.
suggested_fix: Add a test that runs under the contributeur/observateur project: navigate to /fr/reports, verify the 'Avancés' tab is absent or the API calls return 403 (via network intercept).
acceptance_criteria: Test exists that verifies contributeur cannot access the analytics-advanced UI tab

title: e2e/tests/kanban.spec.ts: two unconditional test.skip(true, ...) due to seed-dependency
description: Three tests in kanban.spec.ts skip at runtime when TODO tasks are absent (lines 25-28, 64, 102). While the skip is conditional on data state (not unconditional), the test at line 64 skips with the message 'No TODO task available' and the test at line 102 skips with 'Need at least 2 TODO tasks to verify sort order'. In practice, the E2E DB is reset and seeded by global-setup.ts before each full run (TST-017). If the seed does not create TODO tasks, these tests silently skip in CI. The comment at line 25 ('seed the DB before running smoke') confirms this is a known gap.
root_cause: Test relies on seed data state that may not be present if the seed is incomplete or if the global reset clears data before seeding.
impact: Kanban drag-and-drop tests silently skip in CI if seed data is missing, giving false confidence in green CI.
suggested_fix: In a beforeAll/beforeEach, create a TODO task via the API (POST /api/tasks) and delete it in afterAll. This makes the test self-contained and independent of seed data.
acceptance_criteria: Kanban tests create their own task fixtures via API
No test.skip(true, 'No TODO task available') in CI runs

title: leaves-balance-gating.int.spec.ts: MUTATION WITNESS describe block skipped by default (requires MUTATION_WITNESS=1)
description: The TST-009 mutation witness describe block is skipped in all standard CI runs because MUTATION_WITNESS is not set to '1'. This block is the RED witness for the Serializable isolation test (demonstrates that READ COMMITTED would allow balance overdraw). While the intention (avoid dirtying the shared test DB) is documented, this means the mutation witness — which proves the fix is necessary — is never exercised in CI. If the Serializable guard were accidentally removed, CI would not catch it through this path.
root_cause: Intentional design to avoid ephemeral DB pollution; the env-var gate prevents the witness from running in standard CI.
impact: The 'guard removed' regression would not be caught by the mutation witness in standard CI. The positive Serializable test (not skipped) still validates the correct behavior, so the risk is documentation-level, not correctness.
suggested_fix: Consider adding a CI job that runs pnpm test:integration with MUTATION_WITNESS=1 on a separate ephemeral DB (isolated from the shared integration harness). Document the env var in the CI wiki.
acceptance_criteria: CI has at least one job that runs MUTATION_WITNESS=1 integration tests

title: uploads-auth.hook.spec.ts: OPTIONS preflight test uses .not.toBe(401) — passes on any non-401 status
description: The OPTIONS preflight test asserts only that the status is not 401. The comment explains the rationale (without a CORS plugin, Fastify returns 404 for OPTIONS, and the test only wants to confirm the hook does not inject 401). However, `.not.toBe(401)` would also pass for 500 (an internal error), which could mask a regression where the hook throws instead of passing through. The test is technically correct for its stated purpose but leaves a small gap.
root_cause: The test environment does not register a CORS plugin so a specific 204 assertion is not possible without more setup; .not.toBe(401) was chosen as the minimal assertion.
impact: Very low: a 500 from the hook would pass this test but fail other tests that exercise normal authenticated GETs.
suggested_fix: Add `expect(res.statusCode).toBeLessThan(500)` alongside `.not.toBe(401)` to also reject server errors: `expect(res.statusCode).not.toBe(401); expect(res.statusCode).toBeLessThan(500);`
acceptance_criteria: OPTIONS preflight test rejects 5xx status codes as well as 401

title: 73 migrations total; 50 have no dedicated integration spec (only schema-constraint-named ones covered)
description: 73 migrations exist in packages/database/prisma/migrations. Integration specs (*.int.spec.ts in schema-constraints/) exist for: dat003, dat004, dat010, dat011, dat012, dat013, dat014, dat016, dat017, dat018, dat022, dat023, dat028, dat029, dat032, dat033, dat035, dat036, dat037, dat038, per010, per011, per012, per013 (23 specs). Migrations without any dedicated integration assertion include: dat015 (email length + case-insensitive indexes), dat021 (audit_logs.schemaVersion column + GIN index), dat025 (document FK soft-delete), dat026 (user deletedat shell), dat022 (department FK restrict — NOTE: dat022 IS covered by dat022-department-setnull.int.spec.ts), and the 47 early structural migrations (add_missing_tables, add_task_assignees, add_holidays_and_task_fields, and so on). Early migrations are low-risk (additive columns), but dat015, dat021, dat025 introduce behavioral constraints that have no regression test.
root_cause: The schema-constraint integration test pattern was introduced mid-project; early and some later migrations were not backfilled.
impact: If dat015's LOWER() unique index is accidentally dropped, case-insensitive login uniqueness silently breaks. If dat021's schemaVersion column or GIN index is dropped, the audit query performance and schemaVersion read path break silently.
suggested_fix: Add apps/api/src/schema-constraints/dat015-email-case-insensitive.int.spec.ts that inserts two users with same email in different case and verifies the unique constraint rejects the duplicate. Add dat021-audit-schema-version.int.spec.ts verifying the schemaVersion column exists and GIN index is present (using information_schema).
acceptance_criteria: At least one int spec asserts that duplicate email with different case is rejected
At least one int spec asserts schemaVersion column exists on audit_logs
notes: dat022-department-setnull.int.spec.ts covers dat022. The count of uncovered migrations is approximately 50 (73 total minus 23 with dedicated specs). The high-risk uncovered migrations are dat015 and dat021.

title: e2e/tests/avatar-screenshots.spec.ts: 8 tests with zero assertions (screenshot-only)
description: avatar-screenshots.spec.ts contains 8 tests across 8 pages that take screenshots and write them to .claude-screenshots/avatar-unification/. There are zero `expect` calls. These tests can never fail on a functional assert — only on navigation error or timeout. They run under the role projects (consuming setup dependencies) but provide no regression value. They appear to be debugging artifacts from the avatar unification work (git context: COR-039 web redeploy).
root_cause: Debugging/visual inspection scripts committed as test files without assertions.
impact: These 8 tests consume Playwright project capacity in CI for zero assertion value.
suggested_fix: Either: (a) delete the file (it is a debugging tool, not a regression test), or (b) promote avatar-unification.spec.ts assertions to cover the same zones and delete avatar-screenshots.spec.ts.
acceptance_criteria: No Playwright spec file in e2e/ has zero expect() calls
